source.title: Secure Edge: first-run network canvas & CLI-first install visual assets
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Goal: Produce a cohesive set of onboarding visuals and product screenshots that support a CLI-first private deployment experience for Secure Edge (Redwood Private). This ticket covers: a network canvas storyboard for the operator flow, high-fidelity screenshots for the command-line installer and post-install Console snapshots, an upgrade/rollback visual pattern for air-gapped appliance upgrades, and developer-facing microcopy for each step.

Scope and deliverables: see `assets` list. Visual direction: minimal, operator-focused, emphasize connectivity state and safe-fallback affordances. Illustrate network topology as editable nodes (VPN, NAT, peering) rather than static diagrams so engineers can map their infra during first-run. Avoid marketing-style hero images; favor contextual screenshots + annotated overlays for docs.

Out-of-scope: marketing hero visuals, full localization deliverables (we will provide snapshot-ready text for i18n later), alternate color themes.

Links: Figma prototype (concept + screens): https://www.figma.com/file/secure-edge/onboard-cli-canvas (private); Confluence brief: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/OPS/Secure+Edge+first-run+brief; example GH PR with installer prompts: https://github.com/redwood-inference/private-installer/pull/271

Design constraints and notes: - Screens must be clear at 1200px content width for docs pages and 1440px for Console mockups. - Provide both dark and light background variants for screenshots used in docs. - Ensure terminal screenshots have selectable text overlays (PNG with separate caption file) so docs can copy commands easily. - Upgrade UI mockups must show version diff, preflight checks, estimated downtime, and rollback CTA. - Include a micro-interaction spec (120ms fade for connectivity state transitions, 200ms slide for node expand).

Acceptance criteria: - Delivered Figma file with named frames for each asset in `assets`. - 12 high-res PNG/JPG export images (1200–1440px wide) plus two animated GIFs: one short loop of network canvas interactions, one of the upgrade modal flow. - Copy approved by PM and Engineering (see comments). - Hand-off package in Drive with export pipeline and screenshot naming guide.
assets: First-run CLI flow screenshot sequence (6 frames): installer start, preflight checks, network mapping prompt, key import, final status
Network canvas storyboard (4 frames): blank canvas, operator adds node, operator connects to cloud peer, validation overlay
Console post-install snapshot (2 frames): device list + connectivity detail, routing table view
Upgrade modal and rollback flow (2 frames): preflight + diff view, rollback confirmation and timeline
Terminal copy overlay files (plain text) for all command screenshots
Motion/interaction spec (single page export) and GIF exports (2)
acceptance_criteria: All named Figma frames exported and present in Drive folder with correct naming convention
Screenshots checked for accessibility contrast and terminal text legibility
PM sign-off on copy and sequence (comment thread resolved)
Engineering acceptance on accuracy of installer prompts (linked PR reviewed)
Assets uploaded to Drive + Confluence how-to with embed-ready images
comments: 2026-02-19 - Ava Chen: Kickoff with PM and Infra; agreed on CLI-first requirement and network canvas as the canonical artifact for mapping customer topologies.
2026-02-23 - Diego Ramos: Shared initial wireframes in Figma; asked ENG for exact preflight check list (ENG-3881).
2026-03-02 - Priya Nair (PM): Please include explicit certificate import screen and visual for KMS integration failure mode — added as dependency ENG-4012.
2026-03-05 - Diego Ramos: Iteration 2 uploaded (high-fidelity). Need copy review and accessibility check. Assigned QA for contrast testing.
2026-03-09 - Tomas Becker (Infra): Verified installer prompts match current CLI. Small change requested to wording for graceful-fallback note.
source.qa_notes: QA should verify: exported images match Figma frames, terminal text is readable at 1x and 2x, GIF loops are <=6s, upgrade modal shows preflight failures correctly. Test with macOS and Windows terminal backgrounds to ensure contrast.
source.handoff: Drive folder (design assets + export pipeline): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/secure-edge-design-26412. Include a README.md describing naming conventions: SE-CLI-01.png .. SE-Canvas-04.png and GIFs: SE-Canvas-loop.gif, SE-Upgrade-flow.gif. Add a short Loom walkthrough for engineers (2-3 min).
source.next_steps: 1) Finalize copy with PM (due 2026-03-12). 2) Produce GIF exports and run accessibility checks (due 2026-03-15). 3) Upload assets and request engineering sign-off (due 2026-03-18).
source.notes_for_engineering: Terminal screenshots should be reproducible from installer v0.9.7 (branch: release/secure-edge). We will provide a script to replay installer prompts for capturing consistent images. If installer text changes, update images and notify docs.

source.title: Miniature Request Lens: compact span timeline & correlation scroller
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Goal: Design a compact, embeddable 'Miniature Request Lens' that fits into the Console request list and rollout detail panels for fast triage of problematic requests without navigating to the full trace inspector. The component should surface the most actionable information (critical spans, latency hotspots, correlation IDs, related traces) while preserving alignment with the Console's visual language and accessibility standards.

User problems we solve:
- Engineers and on-call SREs need a lightweight view to triage slow requests inline (list rows, rollout preview) without waiting for the heavy trace canvas to load.
- Product managers want a concise representation to compare pre/post-rollout performance in A/B rows.
- Designers need a consistent compact UI pattern to reuse across dashboards, alerts, and the rollout audit stream.

Design constraints and principles:
- Minimum footprint: target height 136px when collapsed in a table row and up to 320px when expanded in-place.
- Progressive disclosure: show a single horizontal micro-timeline with a density heatline, primary span chips (max 5), and a correlation ID affordance. Expand reveals timeline scrubber and a 'jump to full trace' action.
- Low cognitive load: color usage limited to status (ok/warn/err) and a neutral density palette for non-highlighted spans.
- Performance: SVG-based sparkline + request-level caching so the mini lens renders in < 120ms for typical list pages.
- Accessibility: keyboard focusable scrubber, ARIA labels for span chips, and contrast ratios per WCAG 2.1.

Proposed components (atomic breakdown):
- Micro-timeline sparkline (SVG) showing relative timing, shaded prefixes for cache hits and KV lookups.
- Span chips (compact pill) each with an icon for span type (http/db/kv), latency in ms, and hovercard with a 1-line operation name.
- Correlation scroller: a horizontal scroll area that shows related correlation IDs and their brief status (succeeded/errored/slow) so users can open sibling requests.
- Minimap scrubber: tappable scrub handle that updates a tooltip with absolute timestamps and span focus.
- Quick actions bar: hover-reveal actions for 'Open full trace', 'Pin to rollouts diff', and 'Create incident' (with prefilled fields).

Prototype notes and interactive flows:
- Prototype built in Figma (link below) with two main flows: (1) inline expand from request list, (2) view embedded inside rollout comparison row.
- Interaction: single-click expand, Ctrl+click opens full trace in new tab; scrub updates inline span highlight and triggers a small telemetry call to prefetch the trace payload.
- Mobile behavior: collapse to a single-line summary with the correlation badge and a chevron to full-screen mini-lens modal.

Design trade-offs discussed:
- Density vs. readability: we capped visible span chips at 5 and use a +N overflow indicator to avoid cramming. Some engineers asked for an option to switch to a 'dense mode' for debug sessions; deferred to follow-up.
- Inline caching: prefetching improves perceived speed but may lead to extra network calls; platform telemetry team requested a feature flag to gate aggressive prefetch.
- Visual fidelity vs. implementation cost: proposed SVG sparkline requires runtime sampling of span timings; ENG suggested a fallback static PNG for older browsers.

Feedback and decisions from reviews:
- 2026-02-25 (PM review - Lucia Ortega): "Love the correlation scroller for rollout comparisons. Ensure the quick actions map to the existing rollout audit events schema." -> Added dependency PM-903.
- 2026-02-28 (Engineering review - Rahul Patel): "Prefetch should be opt-in. We'll expose a prefetch config via infra and rely on KV cache headers to avoid extra work." -> Marked dependency ENG-48200 and added performance acceptance criterion.
- 2026-03-03 (Design critique - Nora Kim): "Chip labels need better truncation rules and accessible names. Use explicit ARIA labels with operation + latency." -> Implemented ARIA guidance.

Acceptance criteria (for handoff):
- Figma prototype with interactive scrub and hovercards available and linked; design token mapping completed.
- Edge-case states: empty spans, partial traces, and high-cardinality correlation lists rendered gracefully.
- Performance budget: first-paint of mini-lens <= 120ms on internal staging dataset.
- QA checklist: keyboard navigation, screen reader verification, and visual regression tests.

Implementation notes for engineers:
- Suggested data shape: request_summary { request_id, start_ts, end_ts, top_spans:[{op,type,duration_ms,status}], correlation_ids:[{id,status}], health_status } (note: this is guidance for API contract; final shape to be agreed with ENG).
- Prefetch flag name: tracing.mini_lens_prefetch (boolean).
- Telemetry events: mini_lens_open, mini_lens_scrub, mini_lens_action_click with minimal payload for privacy.

Artifacts and links:
- Figma prototype: https://www.figma.com/file/REDACTED/mini-lens-proto-2026
- Confluence spec: https://redwood.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/CONSOLE/pages/REDACTED/Miniature+Request+Lens+Spec
- Dev PR (placeholder): https://github.com/redwood-inference/console/pull/REDACTED
- Usability test notes: https://docs.google.com/document/d/REDACTED

Next steps:
- Resolve ENG-48200 on prefetch gating and finalize API contract (owner: Rahul Patel).
- Implement first-pass component in staging and run performance smoke test (owner: Marco Alvarez).
- Usability round with on-call engineers and PMs; iterate on chip truncation and action discoverability (owner: Ava Chen).
comments: 2026-02-20 - Ava Chen: Initial concept sketched during design jam. Captured micro-timeline + chip ideas.
2026-02-25 - Lucia Ortega (PM): Reviewed for rollout comparisons; asked for audit schema compatibility.
2026-02-28 - Rahul Patel (ENG): Raised prefetch concerns and suggested feature flag. Referenced ENG-48200.
2026-03-03 - Nora Kim (Design): Accessibility pass; recommended ARIA labels and truncation rules.
2026-03-09 - Marco Alvarez (Engineering): Started spike for SVG sparkline implementation; linked prototype PR draft.
2026-03-10 - Ava Chen: Updated prototype with scrubber micro-interactions after usability session.

source.title: MiniCoach — inline micro-actions for cost literacy during rollout setup
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Problem: users configuring model rollouts and capacity commits often lack quick, contextual guidance about the immediate cost impact of toggles (batching, KV cache, quantization). Large doc-driven onboarding interrupts flow. Goal: design a compact, dismissible "MiniCoach" micro-overlay that surfaces a single actionable tip tied to the control the user is interacting with, with a one-tap action (apply suggestion) and a link to deeper docs. The component should be small, non-modal, accessible, localizable, and trackable for analytics.
acceptance_criteria: Tip overlay appears within 200ms of focus/hover on a rollout or capacity control and never covers primary CTA
Each tip includes concise rationale, estimated token cost delta (approx.), and one primary action (Apply Suggestion) plus a secondary link to docs
Users can dismiss a tip for the session or permanently via 'Don't show again' (persisted in account settings)
Analytics events fire on view, apply, dismiss, and learn-more (events must match telemetry schema)
Component meets color contrast AA and keyboard access (focusable, closable by Esc)
Design spec delivered as Figma file and interactive prototype linked in ticket
source.design_notes: Visual: compact card (360px max width) with icon, single-sentence rationale (<= 100 chars), inline micrometric (e.g., "~10% token cost reduction"), primary button (solid primary, small), secondary text link. Placement: anchored to control's top-right when space allows, otherwise below. Behavior: shows for first two sessions by default; if user applies suggestion auto-annotate rollout as 'optimized'. Localization: allow additional 20% length for translations. Accessibility: aria-describedby on control, role='dialog' avoided; use role='tooltip' when used for hover-only, role='dialog' for interactive action. Edge cases: suppress when in read-only mode or when user has low bandwidth and streaming disabled.
feedback: PM: prefer 'Estimate' wording instead of 'Approx' — clearer for billing conversations
Eng (runtime): request that cost deltas are coarse-grained and computed client-side from existing telemetry buckets to avoid extra calls
Security: avoid exposing exact per-token pricing for enterprise customers — show percentage or tier label instead
Design review: decrease vertical padding and increase hit target on small primary button for mobile
comments: 2026-02-21 — Kickoff: aligned on scope with PM and ENG. Decided to focus first on rollout setup flows.
2026-02-26 — Low-fi comps uploaded; requested cost wording variants from Finance.
2026-03-02 — Usability plan scheduled; target 8 participants (mix of platform and product engineers).
2026-03-07 — Engineering flagged telemetry gap; added dependency ENG-41902 to capture exposure events.
2026-03-10 — High-fidelity updated after accessibility pass; prepping handoff package.
next_steps: Resolve ENG-41902 telemetry contract and update API spec (owner: Kofi)
Run moderated usability sessions and synthesize notes (owner: Lena)
Add localization strings to i18n backlog and reserve translation budget (owner: Priya)
Finalize Figma components and export tokens (owner: Diego)
Create console-2026.03 rollout checklist and release notes entry
source.usability_findings_summary: Initial prototype (n=6) showed 4/6 users took immediate action when 'Apply Suggestion' included a quantifiable cost delta. Two participants wanted an explicit 'why this matters' link. Some users worried about persistent tips adding noise; introduced session-based caps and 'Don't show again' control which tested positively.

source.title: Glassweight token harmonization for mixed-brand consoles and high-density table visuals in low-light mode
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Background: Multiple brand integrations and a recent push toward translucent "glass" surfaces have exposed inconsistencies in dark-mode token mappings. High-density table rows, compact controls, and chart stroke/area visibility suffer when brand accent intensity collides with contrast guardrails. This ticket harmonizes a small, pragmatic token set ("glassweight" family) and defines component-level overrides so partners can opt into brand intensity without breaking accessibility or telemetry dashboards.

Goals:
- Define a concise glassweight token family for dark themes that controls surface translucency, tint strength, blur/opacity trade-offs, and micro-elevation for compact components.
- Map existing brand-accent tokens to conservative dark-mode fallbacks with quantized strength levels (low/medium/high) for predictable behavior.
- Introduce per-component override rules for tables, charts, and compact buttons so visual density and legibility are preserved.
- Provide a migration guide and automated codemod to update console components to the new tokens.

Non-goals:
- Do not change core brand color palettes themselves. This work is about mapping and overrides for the console surface in dark mode only.
- Not addressing mobile platform-specific rendering optimizations (separate ticket).

Success metrics:
- No critical contrast regressions in our eval suite (contrast ratio >= 3.0 for chart lines and >= 4.5 for primary button text in dense table rows).
- A/B test on 5 internal dashboards shows <= 0.5% increase in page render time attributable to visual style changes (target: negligible perf regression).
- Rollout plan accepted and togglable per-tenant in Dedicated/Private deployments.

Tables: zebra and row-hover states maintain text contrast >= 4.5 and chart axis strokes >= 3.0 on target background at 14px text
Charts: default stroke opacity and thickness adjustments applied for heavy-brand-intensity to maintain series separability
Buttons: compact buttons use micro-elevation token; outline vs filled variants choose conservative tint to preserve CTA prominence
Migration: codemod updates at least 90% of console component usages and generates a reviewable diff for manual cases

Design: finalize glassweight token values with contrast calculations and recommended opacities for common backgrounds
Prototype: create a Figma kit with layered components showing light/mid/heavy settings and accessibility annotations
Engineering: add tokens to theme JSON schema and expose a runtime toggle (brandIntensity: none|low|medium|high) in Console SDK
Codemod: author a script that replaces raw color imports with token references and flags ambiguous usages
QA: run automated contrast checks, manual spot checks on dashboard pages, and perf measurement on Electron and web builds
Rollout: ship behind feature flag, enable for internal tenants, collect telemetry, then gradate to wider rollout
notes: Trade-offs: using blur-based translucency on Electron increases GPU work; fallback is a flat translucent overlay (no blur) controlled by glassweight.useBlur boolean
For low-power VMs (Private installs), default to glassweight.mid with no blur and slightly increased stroke weight for charts
We considered a full semantic token rewrite but chose a minimal additive approach to reduce migration surface and risk

title: Glassweight token harmonization for mixed-brand consoles and high-density table visuals in low-light mode
preview:
Background: Multiple brand integrations and a recent push toward translucent "glass" surfaces have exposed inconsistencies in dark-mode token mappings. High-density table rows, compact controls, and chart stroke/area visibility suffer when brand accent intensity collides with contrast guardrails. This ticket harmonizes a small, pragmatic token set ("glassweight" family) and defines component-level overrides so partners can opt into brand intensity without breaking accessibility or telemetry dashboards.

Goals:
- Define a concise glassweight token family for dark themes that controls surface translucency, tint strength, blur/opacity trade-offs, and micro-elevation for compact components.
- Map existing brand-accent tokens to conservative dark-mode fallbacks with quantized strength levels (low/medium/high) for predictable behavior.
- Introduce per-component override rules for tables, charts, and compact buttons so visual density and legibility are preserved.

- A/B test on 5 internal dashboards shows <= 0.5% increase in page render time attributable to visual style changes (target: negligible perf regression).
- Rollout plan accepted and togglable per-tenant in Dedicated/Private deployments.

source.title: Compact template-app onboarding: reference screenshots & README flow diagrams
source.creator: Aisha Raman
source.description:
Goal: Produce a compact set of reference screenshots and a minimal README flow diagram for the Template Apps quickstart that fit within the new docs compact layout.

Context:
- The docs team is introducing a condensed quickstart card for template apps used on the /quickstarts page. We need 6–8 annotated screenshots (desktop + one mobile highlight) and a single-page README flow diagram that explains first-run behavior and environment variables.
- Target audience: developers trying Redwood Inference with a template app (Node + Next.js starter). Focus on clarity, minimal copy, and visual parity with existing console UI (rollouts, model selector, logs).

Deliverables (acceptance criteria):
1) 6 annotated reference screenshots exported at 1440px wide (PNG) and 640px wide (mobile crop) that include: 1) repo clone + .env checklist, 2) local dev server running with Redwood token input modal, 3) console: model selector and latency-cost badge, 4) first call response showing streaming output, 5) rollout toggle + canary sample, 6) telemetry panel with token cost breakdown. Screens should be consistent with Figma component library and use Redwood colors/typography.
2) Single-page README flow diagram (SVG + PNG) that maps: Clone -> Install -> Set ENV -> Start -> Console Connect -> Make First Call -> View Telemetry. Diagram must be export-friendly for docs (800px height).
3) A README snippet (md) with inline images and alt text, copy limited to 120 characters per step.
4) A small captioned gallery for the quickstart card (3 images: hero, streaming, telemetry) sized for fast CDN delivery.

Notes on approach/constraints:
- Keep overlays minimal: use semi-transparent labels for key UI elements rather than full UI mockups. Avoid exposing any real API keys in images.
- Use generic sample content in responses (no PHI).
- Mobile crops should center on the chat stream and model selector for legibility.
- Follow accessibility contrast guidelines for annotation text (AA).

Progress updates / decisions / feedback log:
- 2026-02-12 (Aisha): Draft Figma file created: https://www.figma.com/file/REDWOOD-TEMPLATE-ONBOARDING (internal). Started with 8 screenshot frames.
- 2026-02-18 (Mateo): Reduced scope to 6 reference screens + 3-card gallery to speed up handoff for docs. Added acceptance criteria for image sizes.
- 2026-02-22 (PM - Lina Chen): Confirmed content scope with PM. Added explicit step for telemetry panel. PM requested copy that emphasizes cost transparency.
- 2026-02-26 (Engineering - Javier Ortiz): Flagged that streaming response state may differ locally; recommended using recorded sample JSON and static snapshot to avoid flakiness. Added link to sample response: https://github.com/redwood-inference/examples/blob/main/template-apps/sample_responses/streaming.json
- 2026-03-03 (Design review): Design review notes from Olivia Park: reduce annotation density, increase label font to 12px, and ensure rollouts toggle state is visible at 2x. Action: implement updates and export new assets.
- 2026-03-06 (Dev review): ENG-9072 provided a lightweight mock endpoint that returns deterministic output for screenshots. Confirmed by Mateo.
- 2026-03-09 (Copy review - Samir Patel): Shortened step copy to meet 120-char limit and added alt-text suggestions for each image.
- 2026-03-10 (This ticket): Assets uploaded to Drive and PR opened for docs insertion. Waiting on visual QA from docs team and final signoff from PM.

Links & artifacts:
- Figma master (internal): https://www.figma.com/file/REDWOOD-TEMPLATE-ONBOARDING
- Drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_ONBOARDING_ASSETS
- Sample response (github): https://github.com/redwood-inference/examples/tree/main/template-apps/sample_responses
- Docs PR (draft): https://github.com/redwood-inference/docs/pull/542

Open items / blockers:
- Visual QA by docs (requested 2026-03-10) for image export compression settings (PNG vs WebP).
- Confirm final hero image crop used by marketing for CDN (need marketing input by 2026-03-13).

Acceptance checklist (to be checked off in PR):
- [ ] All 6 desktop PNGs exported and verified at 1440px width
- [ ] Mobile crops exported at 640px width and visually inspected
- [ ] README flow diagram exported as SVG + PNG and added to PR
- [ ] README snippet (md) with inline images and alt text included in PR
- [ ] Gallery captions written and approved by PM
- [ ] Docs PR updated and assigned to docs reviewer

If any of the blockers remain past 2026-03-13, escalate to Lina Chen (PM) for prioritization.
source.notes: Export settings recommended: PNG-24 for transparency screenshots, WebP (lossless) for gallery hero to save CDN bytes. Provide alt text in README and captions in CMS metadata.

source.title: Improve table interaction parity and runtime performance
source.creator: Ariel Chen
source.description:
Problem: Our table component has diverging behavior across density modes and struggles with large datasets. Users reported janky column-resize interactions, inconsistent keyboard focus when using virtualized rows, and confusing filter/sort affordances when density is compact. This ticket scopes a design and token update to deliver interaction parity across density modes, cleaner resize affordances, and layouts that enable engineering to implement efficient virtualization (variable row heights + sticky headers).

Goals:
- Establish consistent resize handles & hit targets across compact/comfortable/expanded density tokens.
- Define keyboard and screen-reader flow for column resize, reordering, and sorting that works with virtualization.
- Create visual and microinteraction specs for sorting and multi-column filters that scale to narrow column widths.
- Provide updated spacing/color/icon tokens to reduce layout thrashing and enable engineering batching/caching optimizations.

Scope excludes: server-side filtering UX, data modeling, and backend pagination behavior (handled by ENG-2101).
acceptance_criteria: Design tokens updated: spacing, touch targets, and icon sizes specified for compact/comfortable/expanded (tokens file + token migration notes)
High-fidelity prototypes for desktop/tablet demonstrating column resize, reorder, sort, and single/multi-column filter flows across densities
Keyboard-only flows documented: focus order, arrow-key resizing modifier, escape to cancel, and accessible labels for screen readers
Virtualization constraints documented for engineering: recommended row buffer, sticky header height API, and guidance for variable row heights
Design review with Eng and PM completed and sign-off recorded (comments addressed)
tasks: Audit current table token usage across components and list mismatches (grid/gutter/padding/token map)
Create new tokens for resize-hit-area, compact-icon-size, and density-border-gap
Design microinteractions for resize (grip, hold-to-drag affordance, snapping behavior) and show edge cases for very narrow columns
Prototype keyboard/responsive behavior in Figma and record a 2-minute walkthrough video
Write engineering handoff: include edge-case flows, ARIA attributes, and measurement points for performance SLOs
Iterate after engineering spike and update prototypes to reflect feasible constraints
comments: 2026-03-02 — PM review (Sofia Patel): Prefer explicit multi-column sort visual state instead of collapsed indicator; need obvious primary vs secondary sort.
2026-03-04 — Eng sync (Riley Thompson): Engineering agrees with token changes but requests a limit on continuous re-render triggers during resize; suggested debounce guidance in handoff.
2026-03-06 — Accessibility review (Jordan Lee): Keyboard resize via modifier + arrow keys is OK but require clear tooltip text and ARIA-live region updates for screen readers. Added examples.
2026-03-09 — Design QA (internal): Prototype shows overlapping filter chips when column width < 90px; added compact filter mode with icon-only chips and tooltip overflow.
2026-03-10 — Mateo (assignee) comment: Preparing handoff doc and PR to token repo; will include a migration plan and codemod suggestions for teams using legacy spacing variables.

source.title: Orchestrated Performance Playbook: Route + Model Correlation UI and Chart Specs
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design a guided performance investigation surface (the 'Performance Playbook') that combines per-route and per-model telemetry into correlated, interactive layers. The Playbook is intended for on-call engineers and SREs as well as product owners to quickly identify whether an incident is routing-, model-, or workload-related and to take safe rollout actions. This ticket covers high-level UX flows, chart-level specs for three canonical correlation views, prototype links, and notes for handoff to frontend and infra for data requirements.
design_goals: Present correlated signals (latency, 50/95/99 latency, token throughput, KV cache hit, batch size) across route and model in a single canvas
Enable temporal scrubbing and span-level annotations (deploys, canary start, autoscale events) so users can link spikes to events
Provide an actionable right rail with recommended mitigations (route reroute, reduce batch, pin model variant, rollback) and one-click rollout links
Keep visuals legible at high cardinality (many models/routes) with adaptive aggregation and on-demand drill-in
Design defensively for mixed deployment types (hosted vs dedicated vs private) and varying telemetry fidelity
user_flows: Flow A: On-call receives alert -> opens Playbook -> sees latency spike overlayed with recent rollout -> clicks 'compare model variants' -> pins older model for A/B replay
Flow B: Product owner wants cost-latency tradeoff -> selects route -> toggles cost layer -> uses 'what-if' slider to estimate cost savings by enabling quantization profiles
Flow C: Platform engineer investigates throughput regression -> switches to per-model view, filters by GPU pool, uses time-range comparison to find kernel-change correlation
chart_specs: Overview timeline (stacked): purpose: global view for selected time range; type: stacked area; series: total requests, successful requests, throttled; x: time; y: request count; interactions: brush to zoom, hover shows route/model breakdown

Latency ribbon: purpose: show distribution; type: layered line + shaded quantiles; series: p50/p95/p99 as lines with inter-quantile shading; x: time; y: latency(ms); interactions: toggle percentiles, add annotation to mark deploys

Token throughput heatmap: purpose: show tokens per minute across routes/models; type: heatmap; x: time buckets, y: route or model (toggle), color: tokens/minute (log scale); interactions: click cell to open sample request list

KV cache-effect overlay: purpose: correlate cache hit-rate with latency; type: dual-axis line (hit-rate) + bar (latency buckets); left y: cache hit %, right y: avg latency; synchronization: aligned x-axis; interactions: threshold alert coloring when hit-rate < configured threshold

Batch-size waterfall: purpose: demonstrate batching behavior contributing to latency; type: stacked histogram showing distribution of batch sizes over time; bucket labels: 1,2,3-8,9-32,32+; interactions: hover to view sample requests and latency percentiles for that batch size

Cost-latency scatter: purpose: per-model points showing average cost-per-1k-tokens vs p95 latency; type: scatter with size by requests-per-minute; interactions: lasso select to create temporary cohort for deeper analysis

Comparison lens: purpose: side-by-side diff between two time ranges; type: mirrored charts (latency, tokens, errors) with change percent overlays; interactions: 'promote to baseline' to update canary comparisons
prototypes: Figma: https://www.figma.com/file/REDACTED/perf-playbook-v1 (contains canvas, right rail actions, responsive states)
artifacts: Related PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/console/pull/4852 (UI scaffold + telemetry endpoints stubs)
decisions: Correlated canvas will default to 'route-scoped' and allow a single-click toggle to 'model-scoped' to keep initial query sizes small
Use adaptive aggregation at >200 unique route/model keys: automatically roll up to 'top N + others' to preserve performance
Right rail actions will map to existing rollout APIs (canary, rollback, pin) instead of inventing new controls
Cost estimates are approximate and driven by a client-side calculator fed with token counts and model unit costs; we will label them as estimates
open_questions: Data frequency: can infra provide per-10s aggregates for latency/requests for hosted and dedicated uniformly?
How to surface private deployment telemetry where KMS and audit logs restrict some metrics?
Do we include synthetic baseline series (nightly regression runs) in the default view or behind a toggle?
What guardrails should the right-rail 'rollback' action require in enterprise orgs (approval, SSO confirmation)?
comments: 2026-02-26 - Luca Bianchi: Initial exploration sketches posted in figma. Requested a cost-latency scatter and a 'compare time ranges' mode.
2026-03-02 - Priya Nair (PM): Added product acceptance criteria and asked to prioritize canary-linked annotations for rollouts. Wants a minimal MVP for 2026-Q1 console release.
2026-03-04 - Diego Morales (ENG): Confirmed backend can surface per-30s aggregates for hosted models; dedicated/private may be per-minute only. Filed ENG-90212 for telemetry infra changes.
2026-03-07 - Security review (notes): No PII surfaced in Playbook; ensure sample request detail is access-controlled. Recommend adding enterprise approval flow for destructive actions.
2026-03-10 - Design review: feedback from design guild to increase visual contrast for heatmap palette and add explicit color-blind mode. Updated prototypes accordingly.
acceptance_criteria: High-fidelity Figma screens for desktop and tablet complete and linked in ticket
Frontend scaffold PR merged with mock data toggles and telemetry endpoints wired to ENG telemetry contract
Three canonical chart specs (Latency ribbon, Token heatmap, Cost-latency scatter) implemented behind a feature flag and validated against production traces
Right rail actions open the correct rollout flows and require confirmation for destructive actions
Design handoff includes component token spec (colors, spacing, typography) and interaction states (hover, focus, selection)

source.title: Introduce expressive-outline variant and compact icon-stack button family
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.summary: Design and spec a new expressive-outline button variant and a compact icon-stack family to improve visual parity across themes and mobile density while fixing recent regression in loading treatments.
source.description: Motivation: recent product requests and QA reports show inconsistent outline contrast across dark/high-contrast themes, and several surface-level regressions where loading spinners overlay icons incorrectly on compact icon buttons. This ticket introduces a new 'expressive-outline' variant (higher stroke contrast + slightly heavier on hover) and a compact 'icon-stack' family that allows stacked primary/icon combinations for dense rows and mobile lists. Work includes token mapping, visual specs for light/dark/high-contrast, updated loading rules (spinner -> inline skeleton or replace icon), Storybook stories, and accessibility verification.
acceptance_criteria: Expressive-outline visual specs added to Figma with light/dark/high-contrast frames
Token names and values documented and mapped to global color tokens (semantic tokens) for light/dark modes
Compact icon-stack family defined with spacing, hit-target, and stacking rules; mobile spacing validated at 320px width
Loading behavior defined: spinner replaces label for standard buttons, inline skeleton replaces icon for compact icon-stack; no visual overlap
Storybook stories for all variants and states (default, hover, active, focus-visible, disabled, loading) merged
Engineering acceptance: CSS variables implemented, unit tests for a11y role/aria attributes, visual regression tests added to CI
Design review completed and signed off by Eng and PM (review comments resolved)
design_notes: Use semantic tokens: button.stroke.expressive, button.stroke.hover.expressive, button.iconstack.padding.xs
Expressive-outline should be ~12% higher contrast than current outline on dark theme; measure against WCAG AA for 14px labels
For high-contrast mode, outline becomes 1.5px inner stroke with 8% increased luminance to preserve separation from background imagery
Compact icon-stack stacks icon above label at <= 40px height, center-aligned; when only icon present, use 44px min hit target
Loading: avoid overlay spinner on icon by either swapping icon with spinner or switching to simplified skeleton animation for icon-only compact buttons
Interactions: focus-visible uses 3px ring (token: focus.ring.thick) to increase discoverability for keyboard users
Include spec variants for 'reversed' contexts (on dark backgrounds) and for when buttons sit on image cards
engineering_notes: Implement tokens as CSS custom properties and map to config in tokens.json for build-time validation
Provide Storybook knobs for theme switching and compact sizing; add Chromatic visual tests for each combination
Add unit tests to assert role=button, aria-busy for loading state, and ensure keyboard activation hits the same focus area
Avoid layout shifts when swapping label <-> spinner: prefer width-preserving skeleton or reserve inline width in compact variants
Coordinate with runtime team to ensure server-rendered HTML uses correct classes for initial loading state to avoid FOUC
Provide migration notes for product teams using old outline tokens (map OLD_OUTLINE token -> button.stroke.expressive)
Estimated engineering effort: 3 dev-days + 1 QA-day for regression testing
decision_log: 2026-02-20: Opted for an expressive-outline semantic token instead of tweaking primary token to avoid broad regressions across components
2026-02-25: Chose spinner swap (replace icon) for icon-only loading instead of overlay due to performance and visual clarity on mobile
2026-03-02: Agreed to use 44px minimum hit area for icon-only compact buttons to satisfy accessibility guidance
2026-03-05: Deferred motion micro-interaction polish to a follow-up ticket to keep this scope focused on tokens and loading regressions

2026-02-22 Diego Martinez: Engineering spike — CSS vars shape looks good; need explicit mapping for high-contrast override. Will prototype spinner swap in Storybook.
2026-03-01 Samir Basu (PM): Confirming this should be shipped before the mobile density rollout. PM approves prioritization as P1.
2026-03-07 QA Lead (Marta Silva): Found a regression where disabled outline still shows hover state in Safari; added a test case and flagged as blocking in PR.

Interactive step tiles: hover/focus states, keyboard navigation, and ARIA roles documented
Export-ready screenshots for desktop/tablet/mobile automated in the release pipeline
Performance budget guidance for hero assets (SVG/PNG size targets) and LCP target <= 1.0s on 3G simulated mobile
Design handoff artefacts and a working prototype link for QA and PM review

source.title: Credential orchestration lab: visual assets for API key scoping, streaming & tool-calls
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design and deliver a compact 'Credential Orchestration Lab' asset suite for the API/SDK quickstart. Assets should teach developers how to: create scoped API keys, enable streaming, and configure tool-calling permissions. Deliverables include static screenshots for docs, an animated micro-demo GIF (streaming token lifecycle), an accessible SVG sequence for key scoping, and code snippet overlays for JS/Python SDKs. Goal: reduce time-to-first-success by making auth and permission concepts explicit with visuals that can be embedded in docs and in-console help.
source.acceptance_criteria: 1) Four export-ready assets: key-scoping SVG (small/large), streaming GIF (looped, 8s max), tool-call sequence PNG frames (3), and one composite screenshot for the Quickstart hero. 2) All images have alt text and accessible captions. 3) Copy approved by PM. 4) Figma file includes slices with naming convention DES-21547_<asset>_v1.png/svg/gif. 5) Assets uploaded to Drive + linked in the ticket. 6) Engineering signoff on SDK code overlays for accuracy.

2026-03-01 DM: Initial draft frames uploaded to Figma (link in artifacts). Focused on contrast for dark-mode and minimal chrome so assets can be embedded in docs without extra CSS.
2026-03-02 PK (PM): Copy edits — simplified language on the key-scoping slide and changed example scopes to 'embed' and 'generate' to match current pricing docs. Approved by PM for developer clarity.

2026-03-03 LO (Eng): Reviewed code overlays — suggested switching the JS snippet to use the streaming cursor API (v2) and adding a short comment about KV cache behavior. Marked as action item for eng docs owner to include a note.
2026-03-04 AC: Accessibility pass — added alt text drafts and ensured color contrasts meet WCAG AA. Noted a fallback PNG will be generated for older browsers that don't support animated SVG.
2026-03-05 DM: Exported assets (v0.9) to Drive and created GitHub PR with updated quickstart markdown referencing the new images. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/docs/pull/4387
2026-03-06 DM: Ready for design review — requesting feedback from Priya (PM) and Liam (Eng) on final copy + code accuracy. Leaving review checklist in Confluence.
2026-03-06 PK: Quick comment — small tweak to the streaming GIF intro text. Approve pending that change.
design_notes: Prefer vector SVGs for the key-scoping diagrams to ensure crispness at multiple sizes; include a 2x PNG export for legacy embedding.
Animated streaming demo should avoid autoplaying audio and be under 8s; include a single-frame poster image for small-screen docs.
Tool-calling visuals show permission modal, sample tool call, and result card — simplified to three frames to reduce cognitive load.
Keep overlays minimal: code font Source Code Pro, 14px, with subtle drop shadow to remain readable over screenshots.
Provide dark-mode variants; use tokenized colors to prevent divergence from product styling.
review_feedback: Priya Kapoor (PM): 'Love the onboarding-first approach. Please replace the 'all-access' example with a scoped example before finalizing.'

source.title: Pulse Inspector: GPU pool health timeline, autoscale tuning & burst policy editor
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Goal: Create a compact, operator-focused inspector panel (codename: Pulse) for Dedicated GPU pools that merges health timeline, topology status, autoscaling controls, and a burst policy editor into a single slide-over UI. The inspector must be glanceable for on-call engineers and also provide safe editing flows for SREs and platform owners.

Scope: initial design covers single-pool view (no multi-select), timeline-based health overview, recent incidents, autoscale target tuning (min/target/max + responsive knobs), burst budget editor, cost impact preview, simple rollback/canary controls, and contextual links to detailed pool logs and metrics dashboards.

Out of scope: full cluster topology management, user-level access controls (handled elsewhere), and deep cost forecasting (only an immediate per-hour estimate shown).
source.acceptance_criteria:
1) Pulse inspector opens via pool row or pool detail header and fits within 980px slide-over width.
2) Health timeline shows 24h/7d/30d toggle with anomalies flagged and one-click jump to logs.
3) Autoscale editor allows safe edits with bounded validation and a 'Preview cost impact' overlay.
4) Burst policy editor exposes budget, cooldown, and priority class with undo/preview.
5) All interactive controls provide tooltips and inline help; design tokens match Console theme.
6) Figma prototypes linked and validated in cross-functional review with Eng and PM.
source.design_notes:
- Visual hierarchy: top area = pool header (name, region, GPU type, allocated/available counts, SLA badge). Middle = timeline (sparkline + stacked event markers). Lower left = autoscale tuning widget (three knobs + recommended profiles), lower right = burst editor (budget slider, estimated minutes at base load). Footer = actions (Save as draft, Preview cost impact, Apply canary, Save & deploy).
- Autoscale knob behavior: direct numeric entry but also '±' stepper for small changes and presets for common workloads (interactive batch, low-latency inference, cost-saver).
- Burst budget: show both absolute minutes and equivalent tokens/sec for context; offer a 'safe cap' toggle that prevents burst from exceeding X% of pool capacity.
- Error states: if a change violates reserved capacity commitments (Dedicated), surface a clear blocker with link to contract/finance.
- Accessibility: ensure color contrast on timeline markers, keyboard accessible sliders, and aria-live updates when preview completes.
- Mobile: slide-over should adapt to narrow widths as a full-screen modal for phone view (primary tasks only).
decisions: Use single slide-over inspector vs separate modal to reduce context switching
Show 24h default timeline but provide quick 7d/30d toggles
Allow immediate preview of cost impact with best-effort estimate; full forecasting deferred to Optimize team
Expose only three autoscale controls (min/target/max) to avoid confusion; advanced configs behind 'Advanced' fold
Burst policy edit is optimistic and requires explicit confirmation for destructive changes
source.comments:
2026-02-21 — Ava Chen: Initial concept walkthrough with PM/Eng. Feedback: show cost delta earlier and add preset recommendations for autoscale.
2026-02-24 — Diego Morales (Eng): Feasibility note: backend provides 1h cost estimate via billing API; realtime per-minute estimates require separate endpoint. Recommend using billing API for preview.
2026-02-26 — Rohit Patel: Updated prototype uploaded (v2) with cost preview panel and presets. Add 'Safe cap' toggle per legal's request.
2026-03-01 — Maya Singh (PM): Reviewed and suggested inline help content for burst policy to reduce support tickets. Also requested a confirmation step for high-cost changes.
2026-03-04 — UX Review (Cross-functional): Action items: a) add undo toast for quick reverts, b) highlight SLA violations before allowing apply, c) produce developer handoff with component specs.

Deliver component spec (sliders, knobs, timeline markers) — owner: Rohit Patel — due: 2026-03-10
Update prototype to include undo toast flows and confirmation modal — owner: Ava Chen — due: 2026-03-07
Coordinate with Billing (ENG-3201) for cost preview endpoint contract — owner: Diego Morales — due: 2026-03-05
Schedule dev handoff and Q&A session — owner: Rohit Patel — due: 2026-03-11
source.notes_for_engineering:
- Provide mock API response shapes for cost-preview and recent incidents.
- Timeline markers should be normalized to UTC in the UI but display local time by default.
- Autoscale edits should be submitted as a staged rollout (canary) by default with ability to apply immediately behind a toggle.
- Ensure optimistic UI updates reflect draft state until backend confirms.
source.review_summary: Design Review 2026-03-04: majority approval from Eng, PM, and SRE. Small changes requested (undo toast, explicit SLA blocker, copy updates). No major UX blockers. Ready for developer handoff after component specs and copy finalize.
source.final_decision: Proceed to handoff once component spec and billing contract are agreed. Targeting console-2026.03 minor release for initial rollout behind feature flag.

source.title: Launchpad empty-state explainer cards and guided microflows
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design a compact, context-aware set of explainer cards and short guided microflows to populate the Redwood Console launchpad (home/dashboard) for new and low-activity users. The goal is to reduce time-to-first-success for new teams, surface high-impact actions (create API key, deploy model, view cost), and provide lightweight discoverability without modal fatigue. This ticket covers patterns, visual language, microcopy, prototype handoff, and a lightweight A/B test plan for 2 variants.
deliverables: hi-fi Figma screens for 2 variants (card-driven vs. checklist-driven)
clickable InVision prototype for usability testing
component spec for engineering (states, responsive breakpoints, accessibility)
acceptance_criteria: Prototype implements 3 principal cards (Get started, Deploy a model, Monitor cost) with clear primary CTA and secondary help action
Interaction states defined for empty, partial, and populated user data
Accessible: keyboard focus order, 4.5:1 contrast for body text, ARIA labels for tooltips
Handoff includes tokens, layout rules, and a minimal CSS token map
Analytics hooks identified for each CTA (track clicks, impressions, completion)
tasks: Run 5 moderated usability sessions on prototype (week of 2026-03-02)
Iterate microcopy and CTA text based on research
Create engineering component spec and deliver to ENG (ENG-4102)
Add analytics events and define funnel in Amplitude
Prepare A/B experiment configuration in Console experiments dashboard
decisions: Prefer inline explainer cards over full-screen tour to reduce disruption (decision logged 2026-02-27)
Primary CTA for Get started will be 'Create API key' while secondary contextual CTAs surface templates and sample projects
Keep initial experiment limited to hosted customers only to avoid private deployment gating
tradeoffs: Cards length: longer cards provide clearer guidance but consume vertical real estate; chosen compromise is 3 concise cards + expandable details
Personalization complexity vs. launch speed: postpone heavy personalization to a later cycle and use simple heuristics (account age, activity)
risks: Users may dismiss cards without completing actions -> mitigate by tracking impressions/clicks and surfacing reminders in the sidebar
Technical: analytics event schema mismatch with current Console telemetry -> require early ENG sync (ENG-4099)
comments: 2026-02-25 Ava Chen: Created initial PRD and exploratory sketches. Captured 6 candidate card concepts from kickoff workshop.
2026-02-27 Marcus Reyes: Reviewed and agreed to inline cards. Flagged engineering constraint: dynamic loading of cards must not add >50ms to initial paint. Assigning ENG sync.
2026-03-01 Priya Kapoor: Research plan uploaded. Recruiting 5 users for moderated sessions, focusing on new signups and dev sandbox users.
2026-03-03 Samir Patel: Architecture note: we should implement cards as a lazy-loaded component with server-side hints to avoid blocking main bundle. Raised ENG-4099 for telemetry alignment.
2026-03-04 Lina Ortega (PM): Reviewed copy. Prefer 'Create API key' over 'Get started' for clearer intent; approved experiment metrics and goals.
2026-03-05 Marcus Reyes: Hi-fi prototypes linked. Preparing component spec for handoff next Monday. Follow-up: verify translations with localization team before experiment launch.

source.title: Cost Investigator component bridge and interaction audit
source.creator: Sofia Morales
source.description: Audit and design bridging components for the new Cost Investigator surface: an exploratory workspace in the Redwood Console that helps customers investigate anomalous spend, correlate traces to model routes, and run quick what-if simulations. This ticket covers component composition, token mapping for contextual states, interactive behaviors (hover/expand/focus), and handoff artifacts for engineering and product. Goal is a single, composable component library subset that can be reused in the dashboard, rollout inspector, and timeline comparison views.
acceptance_criteria: A component inventory sheet (Figma) with named variants for each interactive state (idle, hover, selected, pinned, collapsed, error)
Token mapping document that ties each variant to a semantic token (surface, emphasis, danger, success, overlay) and platform CSS vars
Animated prototypes for row expansion and inline simulation controls with 60ms perceived latency targets
Handoff file with component anatomy, props table, and accessibility notes (keyboard focus order, ARIA roles, color contrast checks)
Sign-off from PM (Asha Raman) and Eng (lead: Priyanka Singh) confirming feasibility for runtime-1.20 release
decisions: Use a single composite 'InvestigatorCard' atom that composes a compact header, timeline sparkline, and action rail rather than multiple bespoke cards to reduce cognitive load and reuse tokens
Prefer semantic tokens over intent color constants for badges to keep cross-surface consistency and enable theme overrides in Private deployments
Favor explicit expand/collapse affordance instead of click-to-expand entire row to avoid accidental activation when scanning results
engineering_notes: Eng ticket ENG-42158 created for web implementation and runtime telemetry hooks
Requested an implementation spike for interaction latency measurement; suggested using synthetic traces from staging to validate batching impact
Proposed API for inline simulation: POST /v1/console/cost-simulate with parameters {route, window, model_variant} — Eng to review security constraints

2026-02-22 Ethan Park: Held sync with Priyanka and Asha. Engineering concerned about initial mount cost for thousands of rows — will prototype virtualization strategy. Suggested deferring pinned-summary micro-animations until after perf spike.
2026-02-26 Asha Raman (PM): Confirmed product priority for anomaly triage flows. Asked design to include a lightweight 'snapshot' export CTA for sharing findings with support.
2026-03-01 Priyanka Singh (Engineering): Reviewed tokens doc. Requested explicit mapping table from semantic tokens to runtime CSS vars and fallback for private deployments missing K8s feature flags.

2026-03-03 Design review: Accessibility pass requested higher contrast for status badges in dark theme and added keyboard mapping for expand/collapse (space/enter). Also recommended motion-reduced variants.

2026-03-05 Ethan Park: Updated prototype with reduced animation timing and added a 'sticky' action rail behavior to keep controls accessible during scroll. Marked ticket In Review and requested QA spike: DES-332208.

source.title: Workspace Sentinel — modular sidebar, route scaffolding, and cross-workspace routing
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design a modular sidebar and routing scaffold to support fast workspace switching, persistent dashboard anchors (cost, rollouts, model telemetry), and progressive disclosure for teams with multiple orgs. Focus on tactile affordances for quick context changes, keyboard-first flows, and clear signal for canary vs stable rollouts. Deliver prototypes for desktop and narrow / collapsed variants, annotated handoff specs, and an accessibility review.
acceptance_criteria: Composable sidebar components documented in Figma with tokenized spacing and states
Prototype supports keyboard nav, command-palette jump, and 1-click workspace pinning
Route schema defined for deep links to dashboards/rollouts/cost-lenses with backward-compatible redirects
Developer-facing spec (paths, query params) added to console API doc
Usability test with 6 PM/SE participants shows 80% success on workspace swap & open rollout view in <= 6s
decisions: Use compact pinned-workspace chips at top of rail instead of full workspace list to reduce cognitive load

source.title: Align typography, spacing, and color ramps for charts and tables
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Purpose: Reduce visual drift across data-heavy components (tables, charts, cards) by consolidating color ramps, tightening the typography scale at small sizes, and standardizing spacing and corner radii. Motivation: recent QA and customer feedback called out inconsistent row padding, low-contrast legends in charts, and varied shadow strength between card components. This ticket scopes token changes (color, type, spacing, radius, elevation), the compatibility strategy, and delivery plan for the March DS release.

Proposed changes (high level):
- Color: introduce a unified neutral ramp for data surfaces (neutral-050..neutral-900) and a separate semantic ramp for data accents (accent-050..accent-700). Increase contrast for legend/text-on-data to meet AA for 12px minimum sizes. Update primary/secondary swatches used in charts to reduce vibrancy clashes.
- Typography: tighten small-size scale: set body-xs 12/16, caption 11/14, table-cell 12/16; reduce line-height variance to improve row alignment. Document token names (type-xs, type-sm, type-base).
- Spacing/radius: standardize table row vertical padding to spacing-2 (8px) and spacing-3 (12px) as toggles; unify card radius to radius-2 (8px) for medium surfaces, radius-1 (4px) for compact controls.
- Shadow/elevation: reduce shadow spread for floating data cards to a subtle elevation (e-1), reserve e-2 for modal stacks; provide a compact shadow token set optimized for 1px device scale to avoid overly soft shadows on small cards.

Acceptance criteria:
- Tokens published to the design tokens repo with clear migration docs and code examples for React components.
- Charts and table components updated in the component library storybook demonstrating before/after visuals.
- Legend and table text meet WCAG AA for minimum 12px sizes in both dark and light themes.
- Frontend integration PRs created with feature flags for a canary rollout.

Rollout plan:
1) Publish token file and mapping guide.
2) Swap tokens in isolated chart + table stories and run visual diffs.
3) Create frontend PR to toggle tokens behind a runtime flag for 10% of users (canary).
4) Collect metrics (error/visual regressions/feedback) for 2 weeks, iterate.
5) Full rollout in design-system-1.9 if no regressions.

Notes on migration: Avoid renaming core tokens used widely (like spacing-1..4). Instead introduce alias tokens (eg. data-row-padding -> spacing-2) and deprecate old names with a 2-release window. Work with ENG to add a compatibility layer in token loader so older releases can map automatically.

Links and artifacts:
- Figma board: https://www.figma.com/file/REDACTED/data-tokens
- Storybook mocks (internal): https://storybook.redwood.ai/ds/data-tokens
- Draft PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/design-tokens/pull/124

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Small text in dense tables may still fail contrast if customers use custom theme overrides. Mitigation: add linting checks to CI and doc guidance for theme authors.
- Risk: Shadow changes may look flatter on high-DPI devices. Mitigation: include device-specific token suggestions and test on macOS Retina and common Android devices.
comments: 2026-02-11 - Priya Singh: Reviewed the proposed neutral ramp. Suggested increasing neutral-700 by 3 L* points to improve contrast in dark mode legends.
2026-02-18 - Mateo Ruiz: Implemented initial token JSON and pushed draft PR. The URL is in the description. Observed 5% visual diffs in table rows due to spacing change — flagged for design review.
2026-02-22 - Derek Morales (Engineering): Asked for an alias strategy to avoid mass renames in services that import tokens directly. Proposed loader mapping approach.
2026-02-25 - Ava Chen: Uploaded Figma replacements and annotated where components should reference new tokens. Calling out Tables V2 and Chart legend components as highest priority.
2026-03-01 - QA (Lucia Park): Ran Storybook visual tests on Linux and macOS Retina. Two regressions in stacked card shadows; agreed to tweak e-1 parameters to reduce blur radius on small cards.
2026-03-01 - Mateo Ruiz: Updated ticket to In Progress and added rollout checklist. Targeting canary rollout week of 2026-03-08.

source.title: normalize-variant-axes-and-auto-layout-prune-for-data-cards
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Objective: Clean up the Data Card component family in the Figma library to reduce variant combinatorial bloat, enforce predictable auto-layout behavior inside dense table/cell contexts, and align naming to the semantic token taxonomy. Scope: - Audit all Data Card instances used in Tables, Charts, and dense list views. - Consolidate variant axes (size / density / state) into a minimal contract that supports: compact, regular, interactive, disabled. - Remove deprecated nested auto-layout frames that cause collapse/flicker when constraints change. - Update token references (spacing, type, color) to use semantic tokens instead of hard-coded values. Out of scope: visual redesigns or feature additions to Data Card content. This ticket focuses on library hygiene and publish readiness.
acceptance_criteria: Data Card component has at most 3 variant axes (size, density, interaction-state) with documented permitted combinations
Auto-layout frames inside Data Card do not collapse when placed in Table cells or horizontal stacks at 320-1440px breakpoints
All spacing and color values reference semantic tokens; no direct px/color hex in published components
Figma library publish passes gating checklist (no orphan layers, naming matches token map, preview pages updated)
A follow-up GitHub PR updates react component snapshots for 3 representative card permutations
steps: 1) Inventory: run instance sweep in Figma to list all unique Data Card instances and note variant permutations
2) Draft a reduced variant contract and post to #design-system for review
3) Remove nested auto-layout wrappers and replace with single predictable stacking frame with explicit hug/fill behavior
4) Migrate px/hex values to semantic tokens using token bridge plugin and update token alias spreadsheet
5) Update component names and variant axis labels to the canonical taxonomy and update library preview pages
6) Run publish gating checklist and create publish PR in Figma; link GH PR to update UI kit in codebase
7) Post-launch: monitor console usage (tickets referencing card regressions) for 2 sprints
decisions: Keep size/density/state as the only variant axes to avoid exponential combinations; remove explicit "compact-with-actions" axis in favor of slot-based composition
Prefer token aliases over refactoring token values right now to limit risk to consuming products; perform deeper token merge in a follow-up
Do not introduce boolean props for every micro-state; encourage composition (named slots) for action areas to avoid variant explosion

source.title: Traffic Inspector & Explainable Rollback microflow — prototype and review
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design a compact, developer-friendly Traffic Inspector panel and an ‘Explainable Rollback’ microflow for the Redwood Console rollouts experience. The goal is to give engineers and PMs a way to: 1) quickly inspect live traffic splits and recent decisioning (why traffic moved to variant B), 2) view the event timeline (errors, latency spikes, eval regressions) correlated with traffic changes, and 3) initiate a rollback with an explanation assistant that recommends rollback scope (full, partial, or target segment) and required follow-ups. This ticket covers low-fidelity flows, an interactive Figma prototype for usability testing with two SREs, and a short design review with Eng to align on telemetry landmarks we must surface in the UI.
acceptance_criteria: Prototype shows a Traffic Inspector panel that surfaces current traffic split, rollout history, and per-variant KPIs (p50/p95 latency, error rate, token cost).
Explainable Rollback microflow provides at least three rollback scopes and a short auto-generated rationale pane (top contributing signals) based on placeholder telemetry.
Design includes a timeline view that links traffic changes to observable signals (errors, latency, eval-score drop) and exposes a one-click preview for canary reconfiguration.
Usability feedback gathered from 2 SREs and 1 PM, recorded as comments and incorporated into iteration.
Design handoff artefacts (Figma frames, annotated spec, acceptance checklist) are attached to the ticket.
comments: 2026-02-10 Ava Chen: Initial sketch complete. Focused on three entry points — rollout list, per-rollout details, and an inline inspector accessible from any route. Attaching low-fi screens.
2026-02-11 Diego Martinez (Eng): We need to ensure the timeline ties into existing request tracing IDs. Will follow up with telemetry owners (ENG-2972).
2026-02-13 Priya Nair: Ran hallway test with two SREs. They asked for a clear ‘reason code’ when traffic switches — adding an explainer pane that summarizes top-3 signals.
2026-02-15 Samir Patel (PM): Love the microflow. Suggest defaulting rollback scope to ‘partial (canary revert)’ for safety. Added note to acceptance criteria.
2026-02-16 Priya Nair: Updated prototype with expandable rationale cards and safer rollback confirmation modal. Shared Figma link for review.

2026-02-17 Diego Martinez (Eng): Design aligns with runtime capabilities except for on-demand backfill of historical evals — ENG-3011 is tracking. Recommend we mark that as graceful degradation (show placeholder and link to evaluation job).
2026-02-18 Ava Chen: Consolidated feedback; moving ticket to In Review. Preparing final handoff artifacts and a short demo for the weekly design sync (2026-02-20).
decisions: Show top-3 contributing signals in the Explainable Rollback pane rather than raw logs to reduce cognitive load.
Default rollback CTA will propose 'Partial (canary) revert' and allow escalation to 'Full' or 'Targeted segment' with one extra confirmation step.
Timeline is anchored to rollout events (traffic change time) and overlays signal spikes — avoids pulling full traces in the first iteration.
Telemetry gaps (historical evals) will be surfaced as a contextual link to the evaluation job rather than blocking the flow.
mockup_feedback: SRE-1: Wanted a clearer correlation line between traffic percentage and error-rate spike; asked for hover-to-highlight across metrics.
SRE-2: Prefer compact card layout for variant KPIs so the inspector fits in a side panel without horizontal scroll.
PM Samir: Emphasize rollback audit trail (who initiated, why) — added to scope for follow-up ticket.
next_steps: Finalize annotated frames and mark ready-for-handoff: target date 2026-02-20.
Host design+eng review (30m) with runtime and telemetry owners to confirm API surface for timeline events (invite ENG-2972, ENG-3011).
Create two follow-up tickets: one for rollback audit trail (PM) and one for historical evals backfill (Eng).
Run a short moderated usability session with 3 engineers using the interactive prototype and capture metrics (time-to-decision, confidence rating).

source.title: Starter Flow micro-screens and contextual screenshots for Optimize: batching, KV cache, and quantization presets
source.creator: Maya Ortiz
source.description: Create a compact set of starter UX assets and product screenshots to live in the Optimize quickstart and release notes. Focus on three core surfaces: 1) Quick presets (Cost-first vs Latency-first vs Balanced) with microcopy explaining trade-offs, 2) Inline UI for per-route quantization presets and a summary chip showing expected token cost delta, 3) KV-cache visual explainer with a toggle and animated prefix caching micro-screenshot. Assets should be designed for small hero placements in the Console, quickstart modal, and documentation thumbnails. Deliverables: Figma screens, a library of annotated PNGs for docs (desktop and mobile widths), micro-animations/GIF prototypes for the caching toggle, and alt text + microcopy suggestions.

Design 6 micro-screens: onboarding hero, preset selector, quant profile drawer, per-route summary chip, KV-cache explainer, preset compare overlay
Create two animated GIFs: KV-cache prefix sketch + quantization slider interaction
Produce PNG exports for docs: 1200x630 (og image), 800x480 (docs inline), 360x640 (mobile preview)
Write microcopy: 3 short pill explanations, tooltip lines, and alt text for each asset

Sync with PM + Eng to validate telemetry labels and copy accuracy

source.title: Resilient mini-spark canvas: semantic prioritization, selective-announcer, and GPU-fallback strategy
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Replace current mini-sparkline rendering for dense time-series with a resilient canvas-first implementation that prioritizes semantic content for screen readers and implements a compact tooltip eviction policy to reduce CPU main-thread pressure. Deliver a pattern that gracefully falls back to CPU-lit SVG rendering when GPU/OffscreenCanvas is not available or when memory pressure crosses a deterministic threshold.
acceptance_criteria: Mini-spark component renders on canvas with identical visual fidelity for low- to medium-density series compared to baseline SVG implementation
ARIA live digest (selective-announcer) emits a concise semantic summary (period, top-3 trends, latest value) within 400ms of interaction for assistive tech
Tooltip eviction policy implemented: tooltips auto-evict LRU for hover bursts exceeding 6 events/sec and collapse to a single sticky summary for >10 series
GPU-fallback behavior tested on Chromium, WebKit, and Electron when OffscreenCanvas or WebGL contexts are not granted
Performance regressions: CPU main-thread time for a 500-line dense view reduced by >=35% compared to current SVG baseline under our synthetic workload
Unit and integration tests cover visual diffing, aria announcements, and fallback thresholds
Design tokens applied for color/contrast and saved in tokens/chart-mini-spark-v2
design_notes: Aim for a single render surface per mini-spark container to avoid duplicated paint passes across stacked lists
Use semantic layers: visual path + interaction hitmap + aria digest generator. The aria digest is not a DOM mirror of the SVG; it is a condensed summary derived from aggregated samples
Introduce a compact announce grammar: <period, direction, magnitude, delta-since-last> to keep live-region payloads short and predictable for SRMs
Tokenize micro-interaction durations: hoverDebounce=120ms, announceDebounce=200ms, tooltipTTL=1600ms
Provide a theming hook that consumes tokens/chart-mini-spark-v2 for colors, stroke widths, and focus halo sizes
tradeoffs_and_decisions: Decision: prefer canvas rasterization to reduce DOM cost; accepts less DOM-queryable paths in exchange for big CPU gains in dense views
Trade-off: choosing LRU tooltip eviction degrades discoverability in extreme rapid-hover scenarios but keeps memory and event handling bounded
Decision: aria digest will be conservative (top-3 signals only) to avoid overwhelming AT users; larger detailed exports available via context menu for power users
Trade-off: OffscreenCanvas + worker rendering improves framerate but increases build complexity and requires careful cross-browser fallbacks
implementation_plan: Prototype canvas rendering + off-main hitmap in a feature branch (design spike) — owner: Marco — due 2026-01-22
Add aria digest generator module and compact announce grammar — owner: Ava — due 2026-01-28
Integrate tooltip eviction policy and sticky summary UX — owner: Marco — due 2026-02-05
Performance runbook and synthetic benchmark harness (500, 2k, 10k series permutations) — owner: SRE/Performance — due 2026-02-12
Cross-browser QA matrix and accessibility audit with internal a11y reviewer — owner: Design — due 2026-02-20

current_progress_updates: 2026-01-15: Spike branch created. Canvas rendering prototype draws multiple series and preserves stroke alignment. Initial CPU numbers promising (-28% on macOS Intel).
2026-01-20: Accessibility brainstorm with a11y lead (Jordan Patel). Agreed to keep announce payload to top-3 signals and add contextual link to expanded summary. Draft live-region sample attached.
2026-01-24: Implemented hoverDebounce and tooltipTTL; found race where rapid enters/leaves create announcement floods. Added announceDebounce and introduced de-duplication layer.
2026-01-29: OffscreenCanvas integration blocked on Electron runtime flag in our desktop wrapper — opened DEP-ENG-44 to coordinate runtime upgrade.
2026-01-31: Design review: PM and Eng concerns about discoverability for keyboard focus. Added accessible keyboard flow that reveals sticky summary on focus with a short delay.
2026-02-03: Began synthetic benchmarks for 500-line dense view. Current CPU main-thread improvement ~33% vs SVG baseline when OffscreenCanvas is used; 18% improvement without OffscreenCanvas.
2026-02-03: Added draft Playbook entry for fallback conditions and thresholds (links in attachments).
review_feedback_and_actions: PM (Lena Ortiz): ensure telemetry events include announceLatency, tooltipEvictions, and fallbackTriggered boolean — action: add telemetry hooks to analytics spec

a11y (Jordan Patel): require screen-reader lab passes on NVDA + JAWS + VoiceOver for both condensed and expanded summaries — action: schedule a11y lab

ARIA digest short-form may omit signals that some power users rely on; need to ensure expanded export is discoverable

source.title: Horizon-band virtualization for long-running series & an a11y-first legend summarizer
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Problem: Time-series charts used in telemetry dashboards commonly display days-to-months of data. Rendering full-resolution lines for very long series kills interactivity and memory, and the legend becomes unusable for dozens of series. We need a design that maintains readable semantics for screen readers, preserves developer-configurable color tokens, and yields a progressive, performant render path across devices.
acceptance_criteria: Horizon-band rendering prototype implemented in Canvas with graceful SVG fallback for <100 points
Legend summarizer component provides an aria-friendly textual summary (series count, primary extremes, last value) and exposes a compact toggle
Performance target: interactive pan/zoom frame <150ms for 20k aggregated points on mid-tier laptop (measured in internal benchmark)
Color token mappings defined for band fills and line overlays; contrast checks pass WCAG AA against surface tokens
Engineering handoff doc (Figma + spec) published and a reference PR linked
comments: 2026-01-10 - Ava Chen: Initial ticket created after design review. Goals: reduce rendering work for long series, make legend usable for high-series counts, ensure screenreader-first summary. Proposed approach: horizon bands for overview + single-point overlays for highlights, legend summarizer that synthesizes top-3 deltas and min/max.

2026-01-15 - Miguel Torres: Spiked a canvas prototype that draws horizon bands in two passes (aggregation -> bands, then overlays). Early results: 20k points -> full-frame render 480ms, incremental update ~95ms. Need to move aggregation to web worker to avoid main-thread GC pauses.

2026-01-18 - Priya Nair (Accessibility): Reviewed aria approach. Recommendation: provide aria-labelledby on the chart that points to a hidden textual summary; avoid solely relying on aria-live for large datasets. Suggested summary format: 'Showing 28 series, timeframe Jan 6–Feb 6. Median throughput 3.2 r/s. Top series X: +21%'. Also require keyboard-focusable legend toggle.

2026-01-22 - Miguel Torres: Updated prototype with worker-based decimation and a progressive render: first draw aggregated bands, then refine visible window. Measured: initial paint 120ms, refinement completed by 260ms in Chrome on M1. Still seeing spikes on low-memory Android devices (Pixel 4).

2026-01-28 - Design review notes: Keep token naming explicit: chart.band.fill.* and chart.overlay.line.*. Avoid single-use color tokens; define token pair (accent, accent-contrast) per palette to support both band and stroke. Decision: use semantic token names mapped in runtime to model tokens for dedicated/private deployments.

2026-01-30 - Eng feedback (Lauren Park): We need a deterministic summarizer algorithm for the legend: input should be series metadata + lightweight stats (min, max, last, name). Avoid computing heavy aggregates in the UI; prefer precomputed rollups from backend when available. Lauren will add a lightweight JS utility lib to compute top deltas if backend not present.

2026-02-02 - Miguel Torres: Preparing handoff. Figma frames updated with keyboard flows and compact legend states. Open items: final contrast checks against dark theme tokens, and fallback strategy for edge-case of >200 series (collapse into 'N others' with expand-by-request).
2026-02-02 - QA note: Plan integration test with synthetic 50k-point series; measure memory and time-to-interactive on CI matrix (desktop/chrome, mobile/chrome, firefox).
