source.title: Retargeting creative kit (Lifecycle Growth Sprint wk3): dev-first variants + exports
source.creator: Ben Carter
source.description:
## Context
We’re launching Week 3 retargeting as part of the **Lifecycle Growth Experiments Sprint** (Hosted API). This ticket produces a small, reusable **retargeting creative kit** for warm audiences (site visitors / signup-no-activation / activated-no-return).

Primary goal: downstream activation lift (First successful request within 7 days), not CTR-only.

## Audience stages (creative should map to state)
- **A: Site visitor (no signup):** “Come back and try it” → quickstart/product page.
- **B: Signup, no activation:** “Finish setup / run minimal request” → console login / quickstart.
- **C: Activated, no return:** “Next value step” → usage dashboard / streaming docs.

## Messaging guardrails (must follow)
- Dev-first, concrete, minimal hype.
- Avoid unverified quantitative claims (latency/cost/uplift). Use directional language ("built for", "designed to").
- Avoid personalization language like “We noticed you…”.
- No enterprise-heavy positioning as the primary headline for self-serve cohorts.
- Ensure CTAs match landing pages exactly ("Get started", "Finish setup", "Open Console", etc.).

Reference: onboarding messaging guidelines + retargeting creatives brief.

## Deliverables
### Concepts (minimum)
Deliver **3 concepts**, each with **2 copy variants** (v1/v2), sized for core placements.

Concept 1 — **Activation flow (Audience B)**
- Visual: simple 3-step flow diagram: Signup → API key → First request
- Copy direction: “Make your first successful request in minutes” / “Finish setup: create an API key”
- CTA: Finish setup / Make first request

Concept 2 — **Cost + latency visibility (Audience C)**
- Visual: product/console dashboard-style frame (illustrative; avoid unreadable micro-text)
- Copy direction: “See token usage, latency, and errors per route”
- CTA: Open Console / View dashboard

Concept 3 — **Dev snippet / Hosted API (Audience A)**
- Visual: text-forward creative with a readable curl or TypeScript snippet
- Copy direction: “Hosted LLM inference you can ship” / “Copy/paste a minimal request”
- CTA: Get started / View quickstart

Optional (only if on time): Concept 4 — **Streaming** variant (Audience B/C)
- Visual: terminal-style “streaming output” lines, high contrast
- Copy direction: “See tokens sooner with streaming”
- CTA: Try streaming

### Sizes
- 1200×628 (landscape)
- 1080×1080 (square)
- 1080×1920 (story) — only for concepts that remain readable in vertical crops

### Export + handoff requirements
- Final exports: PNG (preferred) or JPG, < 1.5MB when possible.
- Provide a consistent naming scheme that matches UTM `utm_content` keys.
- Provide source file link (Figma) with components organized by concept/variant/size.

## File naming (must match attribution/UTM)
Format: `{concept}_{variant}_{size}`
- concept: activationflow | costlatency | hostedapi | streaming
- variant: v1 | v2
- size: 1200x628 | 1080x1080 | 1080x1920

Examples:
- activationflow_v1_1200x628.png
- hostedapi_v2_1080x1080.png

## Acceptance criteria
- At least **6 static creatives** total (3 concepts × 2 variants) delivered in **1200×628 + 1080×1080**.
- At least **one “pure dev” text-forward** variant (snippet-heavy, minimal decoration).
- Text legible on mobile (no tiny subcopy/disclaimers).
- Redwood logo present; product labeled “Redwood API” or “Hosted API”.
- No stock robot/brain visuals.
- Messaging review sign-off (Soojin) + Ops QA readiness (Tyler) before upload.

## Review plan
1) Internal design review (Tessa) — layout + readability.
2) Messaging review (Soojin) — claims + tone + CTA alignment.
3) Growth/Ops QA (Ben/Tyler) — naming/UTMs + platform constraints.

## Open questions (resolve during execution)
- Whether to include any “Hosted → Dedicated/Private later” line: default is **NO** unless there’s whitespace and it doesn’t steal focus from activation.
- Confirm landing URL per audience (needed for CTA exact wording); if not finalized, keep CTA generic (“Get started” / “Open Console”).
comments: 2026-01-24 — Ben Carter: Creating this for Week 3 launch. Priority is a dev-first set; please avoid abstract AI imagery. We’ll map creatives to three audiences (site visitor / signup-no-activation / activated-no-return).

2026-01-25 — Kenji Nakamura: Started in Figma. I’ll keep 2 copy variants per concept; will share first pass as (1) snippet-forward, (2) dashboard/visibility, (3) activation flow. Need confirmation on whether story (1080×1920) is required for all or just top performers.

2026-01-26 — Tessa Morgan: Early comps look good. Request: increase type size by ~10–15% for 1080×1080, remove secondary lines that won’t survive mobile. Keep the code snippet to 4–6 lines max and avoid tiny parameters.

2026-01-27 — Soojin Lee: Copy edits: replace “2-minute activation” with “in minutes” (no hard time claim). Approved headlines: “Make your first LLM API call in minutes”, “See token usage and cost drivers per request”, “Built for production workloads: predictable performance, observability, safe rollouts” (as supporting line only). Avoid “enterprise-grade” as the lead phrase.

2026-01-28 — Tyler Benson: For attribution, we need filenames to match `utm_content` exactly. Please use `activationflow|costlatency|hostedapi|streaming` taxonomy and v1/v2. Also keep safe margins for LinkedIn crops; avoid putting logo at extreme edges.

2026-01-29 — Kenji Nakamura: Delivered v1/v2 for 3 concepts in 1200×628 and 1080×1080. Added story crops for activationflow and hostedapi only (costlatency story was too dense). Shared Figma link in #marketing; exports staged for Ops QA.

2026-01-30 — Ben Carter: Reviewed. These are ready to traffic. The snippet-forward “hostedapi_v2” is exactly the dev-first variant we wanted. Proceeding to upload with conservative spend cap for first 48 hours.
2026-02-02 — Kenji Nakamura: Final clean-up: normalized logo lockup, tightened alignment across sizes, and re-exported to reduce file sizes. Marking done.

source.title: Refresh onboarding email template (v2): layout, typography, and mobile responsiveness
source.creator: Ben Carter
source.description:
Update the HTML email template used for the Hosted API onboarding drip v2 to improve readability, copy fit, and mobile responsiveness across common clients (Gmail, iOS Mail, Outlook). Ensure the template supports A/B subject + headline tests, dynamic sections (by segment), and compliance/footer requirements.

Context
- This template will be used across Week 1 email A/B and subsequent iterations.
- Must align with messaging guidelines: performance + reliability, avoid over-claiming, keep copy developer-first.
- Must not harm deliverability (no heavy imagery; keep text-forward).

Goals / success criteria
- Mobile: no horizontal scroll at 320px; comfortable line length; CTA visible above the fold on iPhone 13/SE.
- Copy fit: supports long subject lines and longer code snippets without breaking layout.
- Accessibility: color contrast, readable typography, link/CTA affordance, meaningful preheader.
- Operational: easy for Marketing Ops to swap modules (hero, bullets, code block, CTA, footer) without hand-editing fragile tables.

Non-goals
- Major brand refresh or new illustration system.
- Rewriting the sequence copy (owned in PM-659 / copy doc); we’ll provide fit feedback + suggested trims.

Deliverables
1) Figma spec for the v2 email template: desktop + mobile layout, typography scale, spacing, component states.
2) Final HTML module guidance (what’s safe across clients; do/don’t list).
3) Copy-fit recommendations: max character counts for subject, headline, subhead, CTA.
4) QA screenshots across major clients (Litmus pass list).

Links
- Confluence design spec (email v2): sources/confluence/product-docs/design-specs/email-onboarding-drip-v2.json
- Messaging guidelines: sources/confluence/sales-enablement/positioning-and-messaging/onboarding-messaging-guidelines-hosted-api.json
- Draft copy variants: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/onboarding-email-copy-variants-v2.json
- Send schedule/triggers: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/onboarding-email-send-schedule-and-triggers.json
source.acceptance_criteria:
AC1: Template renders without layout breaks in Gmail (web + mobile), iOS Mail, Apple Mail, Outlook desktop/web.
AC2: At 320px width, no horizontal scroll; code block wraps or scrolls gracefully without breaking the grid.
AC3: CTA button is at least 44px tall; accessible color contrast; links are distinguishable.
AC4: Footer includes unsubscribe + preferences language, physical address placeholder, and tracking disclaimer per policy.
AC5: Provide copy-fit guidance (subject/headline/CTA limits) and recommended truncation patterns.
AC6: Modules documented for Marketing Ops (what can be swapped, what must stay fixed).
source.work_notes:
Design decisions (v2 template)
- Layout: single-column, 600px max width, centered; 16px side padding on mobile.
- Type: system font stack (no webfonts). Base 16px; headings 20–22px; line-height 1.4–1.6; avoid <14px anywhere.
- Preheader: explicit preheader line to improve inbox scan; hide duplicate content in-body.
- CTA: one primary button; optional secondary text link under CTA. Keep button copy short (2–4 words).
- Code block: light-gray background (#F6F8FA), 12–13px monospace, 8px radius; allow horizontal scroll within code container if needed.
- Images: optional logo only; no hero illustration by default to reduce deliverability risk. If used, keep <100KB and include alt text.
- Spacing: 24px between major modules; 12–16px between paragraphs/bullets.
- Bullets: prefer short bullets (<= 88 chars each). Avoid nested bullets.

Copy-fit guidance (recommended limits)
- Subject line: 35–55 chars ideal; absolute max ~70 chars before common truncation.
- Preheader: 40–80 chars; avoid repeating the subject.
- Headline (H1): target 32–48 chars; max 60.
- CTA: 12–20 chars; avoid punctuation.

QA plan
- Litmus/Email on Acid checklist: Gmail (Chrome), Gmail (Android), iOS Mail, Apple Mail, Outlook 2019/365, Outlook web.
- Dark mode spot check: iOS Mail + Gmail (ensure text remains readable; avoid pure black backgrounds).
- Tracking: ensure UTM/link styling still looks consistent; no underlined CTA button text.
comments: 2026-01-10 (Ben Carter): Creating DES-311 to unblock Week 1 send. Need template that can hold the longer “Hello World” snippet and still look clean on mobile. Please prioritize code block + CTA readability.

2026-01-11 (Tessa Morgan): Pulled in Kenji for layout review. Direction: single-column, text-forward, minimal imagery. Will share Figma spec + do a quick copy-fit pass on top 4 variants from the copy doc.

2026-01-13 (Kenji Nakamura): Copy-fit issue: some variants have 3-paragraph intros before code. Recommend moving code earlier and tightening paragraph 1. Also: CTA labels like “Generate your first response” wrap on mobile; shorter CTA reads better.

2026-01-14 (Soojin Lee): Messaging note: keep headline benefit tied to reliability/latency without “fastest” claims. Suggest ‘Low-latency inference with predictable costs’ over superlatives. Also prefer ‘Run your first request’ vs ‘Start building’ (more concrete).

2026-01-15 (Tessa Morgan): Posted Figma v1 template spec (desktop + mobile). Added modules: intro, bullet proof points, code block, CTA, secondary links, footer. Waiting on Marketing Ops to confirm which editor constraints we need to respect.

2026-01-16 (Tyler Benson): Ops constraints: we can support modular sections but need table-based layout; avoid complex CSS selectors. Preheader is supported. Please include guidance on safe CSS + a ‘no-go’ list (position:fixed, background images, etc.).

2026-01-18 (Tessa Morgan): Updated spec to table-safe patterns + tightened spacing for Outlook. Added explicit min button height and enlarged tap targets. Included copy-fit limits in the spec so Ben can trim variants proactively.
2026-01-20 (Marissa Cole): Quick FYI—console nudge CTA uses same phrasing as Email #1. Please keep CTA consistent: ‘Make your first request’. This will reduce confusion when users see both surfaces.

2026-01-21 (Tessa Morgan): Ran Litmus pass: Gmail/iOS Mail/Apple Mail good; Outlook desktop needed padding adjustments (moved padding from div to td). Code block now contains its own scroll region on small widths. Marking ready for implementation.
2026-01-23 (Ben Carter): Shipped with Email A/B launch. Template looks solid in test sends. Closing DES-311; follow-ups can be tracked as tweaks in PM-659 if we need additional module variants.

text:
Update the HTML email template used for the Hosted API onboarding drip v2 to improve readability, copy fit, and mobile responsiveness across common clients (Gmail, iOS Mail, Outlook). Ensure the template supports A/B subject + headline tests, dynamic sections (by segment), and compliance/footer requirements.

Context
- This template will be used across Week 1 email A/B and subsequent iterations.
- Must align with messaging guidelines: performance + reliability, avoid over-claiming, keep copy developer-first.
- Must not harm deliverability (no heavy imagery; keep text-forward).

Goals / success criteria
- Mobile: no horizontal scroll at 320px; comfortable line length; CTA visible above the fold on iPhone 13/SE.
- Copy fit: supports long subject lines and longer code snippets without breaking layout.
- Accessibility: color contrast, readable typography, link/CTA affordance, meaningful preheader.
- Operational: easy for Marketing Ops to swap modules (hero, bullets, code block, CTA, footer) without hand-editing fragile tables.

Non-goals
- Major brand refresh or new illustration system.
- Rewriting the sequence copy (owned in PM-659 / copy doc); we’ll provide fit feedback + suggested trims.

Deliverables
1) Figma spec for the v2 email template: desktop + mobile layout, typography scale, spacing, component states.
2) Final HTML module guidance (what’s safe across clients; do/don’t list).
3) Copy-fit recommendations: max character counts for subject, headline, subhead, CTA.
4) QA screenshots across major clients (Litmus pass list).

source.title: Chronicle Atlas: Micro-journey Mapping & Cost Lensing Prototype
source.creator: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Goal: Prototype a compact, context-aware navigation overlay (Chronicle Atlas) that surfaces micro-journeys across Console surfaces (Dashboards, Rollouts, Cost Views) and folds in a lightweight cost-lensing control. The Atlas is intended to reduce cognitive load when switching contexts, surface relevant rollouts and cost metrics inline, and provide predictable routing behavior for multi-workspace customers. Scope: IA sketch -> mid-fidelity prototype -> internal usability session -> iterate on placement/interaction. Out of scope for this ticket: backend telemetry plumbing, RBAC enforcement, and Dedicated deployment config view changes (these are follow-ups tracked under ENG tickets).
acceptance_criteria: Clickable prototype in Figma showing Atlas open/close, journey selection, and cost-lensing toggle for Dashboard/Release/Cost routes
Design review approval from PM (Sofia Lin) and Eng lead (Jordan Kim) documented in comments
Usability notes from at least 4 internal sessions captured and follow-up issues created
Annotated navigation spec (Confluence) mapping old routes to new Atlas abstractions and fallback behavior
design_notes: Prioritized keyboard-first interactions (Cmd+K to open Atlas) to preserve discoverability for power users
Compact card layout chosen to avoid pushing existing topnav; cards contain 1-line breadcrumb, primary metric (latency/cost), and action affordance
Cost-lensing is a transient overlay that queries cached cost summaries; default off to avoid noisy billing info in primary flows
Trade-off: embedding rollout quick-actions into the Atlas speeds recovery from incidents but adds surface area to the nav - mitigated by progressive disclosure and permission gating
Considered variant where Atlas replaces left rail for low-density users; rejected for this cycle due to telemetry gaps and migration risk

2025-11-16 - Mateo: Mid-fi Figma prototype v0.2 shared. Noted issues with focus trapping when Atlas is open; flagged for accessibility pass (ENG-2641).
2025-11-18 - Sofia Lin (PM): Love the cost-lens idea; requested that we surface 7-day rolling spend and a quick 'estimate-savings' CTA when applying recommended batching/quantization suggestions.
2025-11-19 - Jordan Kim (Eng): Feasibility check: embedding rollout quick-actions is doable but will require a small API to fetch canary statuses; ENG-1987 opened to track work.
2025-11-20 - Usability Session 1 notes: testers were confused by similar icons for workspace switching and Atlas open; action: explore distinct affordances and a slight animation to emphasize source.
2025-11-22 - Priya: Iterated prototype (v0.4) with updated iconography and reduced card height; uploaded to Figma link. Added keyboard shortcuts to spec.

2025-11-24 - Design Review: PM Sofia and Eng Jordan approved direction pending accessibility remediation and adding analytics events for 'atlas_open' and 'cost_lens_toggle' (tracked under ENG-2641 and ENG-2715).

2025-11-25 - Mateo: Created follow-up tickets for telemetry (ENG-2715), backend cost summaries (ENG-2733), and rollout quick-actions backend (ENG-1987). Next: schedule cross-functional design handoff and eng grooming in Sprint 2025-W48.

source.title: Compact aggregate announcer + render-decimation for high-density time-series
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem:
High-density time-series views (dashboards with many small series or very long histories) are causing two user-facing issues: 1) screen-reader consumers get overwhelmed by thousands of live announcements when the chart updates, and 2) interactive latency spikes (hover/tooltip + legend updates) as DOM nodes and per-series layers multiply.

Goal:
Design a lightweight, accessible pattern and visual affordance that: 1) collapses non-essential series into an ARIA-friendly summary that screen readers can consume without noise, 2) preserves discoverability and keyboard navigation for power users, and 3) reduces DOM and paint overhead to improve interactive latency for common dashboard workloads.

Constraints:
- Must be implementable without breaking existing SDK consumers of chart components.
- Must respect tokenized color and spacing system (so theme changes cascade).
- Should provide clear fallback for low-density mode (no change to current experience).
- Must meet Redwood console accessibility baseline and SLOs for interaction latency.

Proposed solution (high level):
1) Compact Aggregate Announcer: an opt-in chart mode that groups low-importance series into a single "aggregate" cluster. For assistive tech we expose a single ARIA region summarizing the hidden series (count, min/max/last value, top-changed series) and an explicit "expand" control that keyboard + SR users can trigger to enumerate items on demand.
2) Render-decimation pipeline: introduce a rendering pre-pass that computes a tile-level density map and decides per-tile rendering strategy: full vector for focused tiles, decimated canvas raster for background tiles, and a static image snapshot for extremely dense/idle tiles. This reduces per-series repaint and DOM churn.
3) Legend & tooltip strategy: replace per-series legend DOM nodes with a virtualized legend list. Tooltips for hidden/aggregated series are surfaced in the aggregate summary panel rather than as individual popovers.
4) Token alignment: add two new design tokens (chart.aggregation-badge-color, chart.aggregate-announcer-font-size) and update the compact legend token set to ensure visual parity between expanded and collapsed states.

Trade-offs considered:
- Hiding series by default reduces discoverability for novice users. Mitigation: show visually prominent soft-hint ("N collapsed") with one-click expand, and keep expand control in keyboard tab order.
- Rasterization sacrifices crispness for performance on extreme zooms. Mitigation: prefer vector for focused tiles and only rasterize background tiles; provide device-pixel-ratio-aware snapshots.

Success metrics / acceptance criteria:
- Screen reader tests: SR walkthrough of a 200+ series chart yields a single concise summary read by NVDA/VoiceOver by default, with an expand action that reads per-series items on demand.
- Performance: for a synthetic dashboard with 5K series (resampled to display resolution), interactive hover latency (time between mousemove and visible hover highlight update) improves by >= 40% compared to baseline.
- DOM nodes: per-chart DOM nodes reduced by >= 70% for high-density views.
- Visual parity: default view for under-20-series charts is identical to current UX.

Deliverables:
- Design mockups (compact + expanded states) and microinteraction spec (enter/exit aggregate, keyboard flows).
- New token suggestions and token migration notes for design system owner.
- Prototype: interactive Figma with overlay annotations + small React prototype integrating virtualized legend list.
- Accessibility test plan and scripts for manual SR testing.

Acceptance checklist:
- [ ] Mockups reviewed and approved by PM (Lina Gomez) and Engineering lead (Mateo Ruiz).
- [ ] Token PR submitted to design-tokens repo with fallback tokens.
- [ ] Prototype PR with smoke perf measurements attached.
- [ ] Usability session with 4 participants including 1 screen-reader user scheduled and completed.

Notes on related work:
- This ticket intersects with ENG-21012 (rendering pipeline refactor) and PM-502 (dashboard aggregation UX). Coordinate pre-deploy contract with runtime team to avoid double work.
comments: 2025-11-04 Ava Chen: Initial draft of concept. Shared early with console PMs and runtime eng to validate feasibility. Uploaded Figma frames: https://www.figma.com/file/XYZ/compact-aggregate-announcer

2025-11-07 Mateo Ruiz (Engineering): Feasibility note — render-decimation idea aligns with our tile-culling work. Concern about snapshot invalidation frequency; suggested TTL-based cache and GPU-friendly canvas layers. See ENG notes: https://confluence.redwood.ai/ENG/rendering/tile-culling

2025-11-10 Lina Gomez (PM): +1 on the accessibility-first approach. Request to surface aggregated metrics (sum/mean/stddev) in the announcer so business users can quickly triage. Marking metric set in acceptance criteria.

2025-11-14 Design review: Ran quick hallway test with two designers. Feedback: make expand affordance color token-driven, and ensure keyboard focus ring is 3px per token guidelines. Updated tokens list in attachments.

2025-11-19 Noah Patel (Assignee): Prototype PR opened with virtualized legend and aggregate panel: https://github.com/redwood-inference/console/pull/1423 — attaching early perf numbers in PR comments (hover latency down ~38% on dev box). Will run server-side perf harness next week.

source.title: Workspace Spotlight & Contextual Surface: navigation prototype for dashboards, rollouts, cost views
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Problem: The Console navigation is fragmented for users who need quick access to operational surfaces (dashboards, rollouts, cost breakdowns) across multiple workspaces. Current left-rail mixes global product routes with workspace-scoped pages leading to routing ambiguity and slower task completion. Goal: Design a compact, discoverable navigation pattern that surfaces workspace-scoped destinations (dashboards, rollouts, cost views) without increasing cognitive load for occasional users, and that supports fast workspace switching for power users.
source.context: Stakeholders: Console PM (Diego Ramirez), Eng leads (Liam O'Connor), Reliability (Monica Patel). Constraints: must integrate with existing route structure, preserve deep links for analytics, and be keyboard-accessible. Must work with both hosted and Dedicated/Private orgs where workspace names and permissions vary.
source.proposed_solution: Introduce a 'Workspace Spotlight' micro-surface in the left rail: a lightweight, collapsible module showing 4 pinned workspace-scoped routes (Overview, Dashboards, Rollouts, Cost). Spotlight is context-aware (switches content based on active workspace) and supports an instant 'spotlight' keyboard shortcut (Cmd+K-like) for fuzzy jump. Add a secondary compact workspace switcher that preserves current route when changing workspaces. Provide an optional expanded rail variant for power users that surfaces route counts and latency badges.
design_artifacts: GitHub PR - initial implementation of micro-rail: https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-console/pull/2384
acceptance_criteria: Spotlight module appears in left rail and shows the four workspace-scoped routes for the active workspace
Switching workspace via spotlight switcher preserves the current route when the destination exists in the target workspace, otherwise falls back to Overview
Cmd/Ctrl+K opens fuzzy jump with workspace-aware suggestions that prioritize Dashboards, Rollouts, Cost views
Telemetry events emitted for spotlight impressions, workspace switches, and fuzzy-jump selections
Usability test: median time-to-target reduced by >=20% for tasks accessing Rollouts or Cost views
notes: 2025-11-04: Kickoff — scoped feature, aligned on acceptance criteria with PM and eng. Decided to limit initial scope to four pinned routes.
2025-11-07: Low-fi sketches -> two variants: inline micro-rail vs overlay quick-spotlight. Chose inline for lower discoverability cost and analytics continuity.
2025-11-10: Accessibility review — ensured 1) spotlight focus trap, 2) proper aria labels for collapsed/expanded states, 3) keyboard navigation between pinned items.
2025-11-12: Design review with Eng — raised concerns about route preservation semantics when workspaces have mismatched feature sets. Proposed fallback to Overview and tracked as ENG-2109.
2025-11-15: Prototype user testing (6 participants) — found success on quick access but confusion about pinned item labels; updated CTAs to include icons + short labels and added hover tooltips.
2025-11-18: PM review — requested metrics instrumentation and an opt-out for orgs that prefer legacy rail. Added telemetry checklist and admin toggle in design.
2025-11-19: Submitted final artifacts and requested engineering review; PR created for micro-rail scaffolding. Moving to In Review.
tradeoffs: Discoverability vs. density: The micro-rail reduces clicks for frequent tasks but slightly increases left-rail real estate. Compensated by making the module collapsible and allowing admins to opt-out.
Global routing vs. workspace scope: Preserving current route on workspace switch is simpler for users but requires feature-gating knowledge on backend; fallback logic required for missing routes.
Incremental rollout: Implementing as an opt-in flag allows telemetry-driven rollout but delays immediate adoption for power users.
review_feedback: PM (Diego): Add cost-specific microcopy that clarifies why a user would go to Cost (billing vs. per-route cost breakdown).
Eng (Liam): Need a clear contract for route resolution across workspaces. Filed ENG-2109 to define edge cases and data APIs.
SRE (Monica): Add a fallback to server-side redirect when client-side route resolution fails to avoid 404s during workspace migrations.
Design lead (Maya Singh): Suggest adding a lightweight onboarding tooltip the first time Spotlight appears to explain workspace-scoped routes.
next_steps: Sync with ENG-2109 to finalize route resolution API (assigned to Liam) — target 2025-11-22
Finalize Figma frames for expanded rail variant and handoff tokens (Priya) — target 2025-11-21
Add telemetry events and create dashboard to track adoption (Diego + Analytics) — target 2025-12-05
Prepare a small A/B test for opt-in rollout and collect usability metrics over 2 weeks post-launch

source.title: Span Heatmap & Correlation Explorer for Request Drilldown
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design a compact, scannable request drilldown view that surfaces span-level heatmaps, correlation IDs, and timeline analytics to help engineers quickly identify tail-latency contributors, model-variant fallbacks, and retry patterns. This explores a dual-pane layout: a left pane for request metadata + filters and a right pane for an interactive waterfall with a span heatmap overlay and resource usage annotations.
source.design_notes: Key design goals: 1) fast triage — let engineers find problematic requests within 3 clicks; 2) temporal context — show relative timings and sequence of spans with clear parent/child relationships; 3) correlation surfaces — surfacing correlation IDs to link traces across services and model variants; 4) graceful performance — render large traces progressively with virtualization and summary rows. Avoid duplicating full trace inspector UI; this is focused on operational triage for the Redwood Console.
acceptance_criteria: Left pane shows request metadata: correlation ID, model variant, route, timestamp, token counts and failure flags
Right pane renders waterfall with span rows and a colored heatmap representing latency percentile buckets (p50/p95/p99)
Hovering a span expands an inline detail card with key attributes (cpu/gpu usage if available, input size, function call info) and links to related logs/PRs
Filters include time range, status (error/warn/success), model variant, hostname/region, and correlation ID search
UI supports keyboard navigation between spans and collapsible parent spans for deep traces
Performance: UI supports traces up to 20k spans with progressive loading and shows an aggregated summary when full load is disabled
All screens meet AA color contrast and have accessible labels for interactive controls
review_comments: PM (Diego Morales): please add a way to pin a correlation ID to the top-level dashboard for alerts
Eng (Priya Nambiar): prefer lightweight heatmap binning (10 buckets) and server-side summarization for large traces
Infra (Liam O'Connor): need a fallback view for traces missing resource metrics; show 'metric unavailable' states
Design (Sofia Patel): consider an inline mini-timeline sparkline in the left pane for quick visual prioritization
decisions: Use heatmap overlay aligned to waterfall rather than separate chart to keep visual context
Progressive rendering: initial summary + first 200 spans then lazy-load children on expand
Expose correlation ID pinning as a lightweight action that creates a console bookmark (deferred to PM for persistence implementation)
Accessibility: keyboard-first controls and explicit aria-labels for span controls
next_steps: Incorporate Eng feedback about server-side summarization and update prototype with aggregated bins
Create a handoff packet (specs, tokens, example API responses) and sync with ENG-2148
Schedule a design review with PM and SRE on 2025-06-12
Run a 1:1 usability test with two backend engineers to validate triage flow by 2025-06-16
accessibility_considerations: Ensure all interactive elements reachable and operable via keyboard
Provide text equivalents for heatmap intensities and offer an option to switch to numeric view
Ensure focus order reflects the logical triage flow (filters -> list -> waterfall -> span detail)
implementation_considerations: Eng to implement server-side aggregation endpoint that returns span buckets and top N slow spans
Client virtualization for waterfall rows (react-window or equivalent) to handle large traces
Telemetry: capture UI interactions for heatmap bin toggles and correlation pin actions for future product metrics
Auth: respect per-tenant RBAC; hide raw resource metrics when not permitted
mockup_versions: v2 - interactive hover cards + correlation panel (2025-05-28)

source.title: Platform interaction anomalies and token regression review for responsive UI kit
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Goal: Run a focused cross-platform QA pass on the UI kit to identify responsive and cross-browser regressions introduced by recent token and component changes (tokens v2 merge + button polymorphism refactor).

Scope: buttons, interactive states, table headers/sticky behavior, responsive table collapse, chart rendering at tight widths, and global token fallbacks (color, spacing, radius). Test matrix covers Desktop (Chrome/Firefox/Safari latest), iOS Safari, Android Chrome, and viewport breakpoints for mobile/portrait/landscape.

Deliverables: concise triage list with reproducible steps, prioritized ENG tickets for fixes, design patches (token updates or fallback suggestions), and a small acceptance harness (screenshots + Percy/Chromatic notes) for follow-up regression checks.
source.acceptance_criteria:
1) Each reported issue has at least one reproducible test case (steps + screenshot).
2) Priority P0/P1 issues have an ENG ticket or clear owner assigned.
3) Design proposes token-level remediation for visual regressions (e.g., alpha blend fallback or explicit token mapping).
4) Mobile layout regressions have a suggested responsive CSS pattern or component-level patch.
5) QA checklist and test matrix are added to the Design System docs and linked from the ticket.
source.observed_issues_summary:
- Safari (macOS & iOS): button focus rings shift by 1px on high-DPI screens after tokens v2; now inconsistent when -webkit-font-smoothing differs.
- Firefox (desktop): small-button hit area reduces by computed padding collapse in nested flex containers; causes tap misses when used in toolbar patterns.
- Mobile (iOS landscape): table header sticky positioning breaks when device safe-area inset changes during orientation; header overlaps first row.
- Charts (tight widths): axis label truncation differs across browsers; Firefox drops fractional subpixel rounding leading to label jitter.
- Tokens: color token --ui-surface-0 resolves to different hex on Safari stable vs Canary due to alpha flattening; affects contrast and accessible text color calculations.
source.next_steps:
1) Create ENG tickets for top 4 regressions (ENG-4120, ENG-4121, ENG-4122, ENG-4123).
2) Attach repro builds and Percy snapshots for each.
3) Design to propose token fallback map and small CSS shim for Safari: add explicit transform/translate to avoid subpixel rounding artifacts.
4) Prioritize fixes in next sprint for P0/P1.
5) Add automated visual test coverage for the toolbar/button and table-sticky scenarios in Chromatic.
6) Schedule a short sync with Runtime team to discuss chart rendering inconsistencies (proposed meeting: 2025-04-12).
updates: 2025-04-02: Kickoff - Reviewed token v2 merge and recent button refactor; created initial test matrix and baseline screenshots for Chrome (Ava).
2025-04-04: Discovered Safari focus ring offset on M1 devices; reproduced on both Safari 16 and iOS 16 (Mateo). Filed initial notes and capture in Confluence.

2025-04-06: Firefox hit-area issue triaged — reproduced in nested flex toolbars with collapse-to-icon behavior. Confirmed this is not present in Chrome; likely difference in computed padding rounding (Design + Eng sync scheduled).

2025-04-08: Chart truncation repro added with specific dataset and viewport sizes; attached a minimal reproduction repo to the related_prs list. Decided to request Runtime input on subpixel rendering and axis canvas vs SVG trade-off.

2025-04-10: Prioritization call - marked Safari focus ring and Firefox hit-area as P0; table sticky and chart jitter as P1. Created placeholder ENG tickets and assigned owners. Preparing patches for token fallbacks.
source.notes: Trade-offs discussed: lightweight CSS shims vs changing base tokens. Prefer token-level fix when possible to avoid multiple component work, but will use targeted shims for urgent P0 visual regressions. Need to coordinate any token changes with SDK and runtime teams to avoid cascading regressions.

Deliverables: concise triage list with reproducible steps, prioritized ENG tickets for fixes, design patches (token updates or fallback suggestions), and a small acceptance harness (screenshots + Percy/Chromatic notes) for follow-up regression checks.

source.title: Migrate legacy interaction tokens and audit keyboard landmarks for assistive mode
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Phase 1 effort to migrate deprecated interaction tokens used across buttons, form controls, table rows, and chart legends; perform a focused keyboard-landmarks audit for the new assistive/compact mode; and deliver conformance artifacts and a lightweight VPAT-style package for product teams.
acceptance_criteria: All legacy interaction tokens (x-interaction-*) replaced with new semantic tokens in token-2.0 mapping
Contrast re-mapping for hover/active/focus states validated with automated color-check scripts and manual spot checks for chart legends and dense tables
Keyboard landmark/reporting audit completed for assistive mode with remediation tickets created for each failing component
Design artifacts and README with token migration guide delivered to repo and shared with Engineering and PM
VPAT-style conformance summary drafted and reviewed by Accessibility SME and Product Compliance
notes: We scoped this ticket to interaction tokens and keyboard landmarks only — separate follow-ups will handle live-region semantics and screenreader phrasing across complex widgets.
Aim is minimal visual regression: prefer token remapping with component-level overrides instead of style rewrites where possible.
We will use automated contrast checks (axe-core color contrast and pa11y) plus a small manual verification matrix for edge cases (mini charts, dense tables, pill sets).
comments: 2025-03-04 Ava Chen: Initial ticket created after weekly accessibility sync. Focus on tokens that power hover/focus outlines and dense control contrast.
2025-03-06 Maya Patel: Completed inventory of components using legacy tokens — list attached. Noticed inconsistent use in chart legend pills and compact table rows.
2025-03-10 Marcus Rivera (Eng): Proposed token alias layer in runtime to allow safe rollout without immediate component PRs — added to dependencies ENG-20510.
2025-03-12 Accessibility Review: Small set of ARIA role mismatches found in table row actions and nested menus; created remediation tickets DES-208912 and DES-208913.
2025-03-16 Maya Patel: Uploaded migration guide and contrast matrix. Started implementation notes for developers — see links.
2025-03-20 Jenna Ortiz (PM): Reviewed draft VPAT-style summary; requested clearer language around scope and limitations for Large Enterprise customers.
decisions: Adopt token alias layer first (runtime support) to reduce churn in component PRs — agreed 2025-03-10.
Prioritize keyboard-landmark fixes that block keyboard-only discovery flows before visual contrast changes.
VPAT-style package will be scoped to changes introduced by this migration only and will include a clear list of unaddressed widgets.
qa_steps: Run automated contrast checks on updated token set using axe-core and custom color-tooling
Manual keyboard walkthrough across Home Dashboard, Shared Table, and Mini Chart embed in assistive mode
Screen reader smoke test (NVDA/VO) for table row actions and legend pill focus order
Confirm no pixel-diff regressions on high-frequency pages using Percy snapshots with token overrides
next_steps: ENG: land runtime alias layer PR (ENG-20510) to allow soft swap of tokens — blocker until merged
DES: publish 'token-migration.md' in repo root and link to component migration checklist
PM: coordinate with compliance for VPAT summary review (due 2025-03-28)
QA: schedule 2-hour manual regression session for assistive mode on 2025-03-26

source.title: Cluster Guardian: health-orbit canvas & burst orchestration for dedicated fleets
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design a consolidated 'Cluster Guardian' canvas for Dedicated capacity that surfaces real-time health signals, predictive stress windows, and an orchestration panel for temporary burst allocations. The goal is to give SREs and platform engineers a single surface to assess fleet resiliency, tune autoscaling policies, and authorize short-lived burst budgets with clear cost and latency trade-offs. Deliverables: high-fidelity Figma prototype, interaction spec for rollouts & fallback, a small component library (status orb, ledger rows, resilience lanes), and a handoff checklist for engineering and product. Focus areas: (1) health-orbit visualization summarizing per-pool KPIs without overwhelming rows of metrics, (2) an autoscaling policy composer that previews expected throughput/latency under selected rules, (3) a burst orchestration flow including request, approve, and rollback with cost impact preview, (4) mobile/compact variants for on-call quick triage.
comments: 2025-03-12 Ava Chen: Kickoff — scoped canvas to three primary lanes: health summary, policy composer, burst ledger. Included acceptance criteria and success metrics. Linked initial PRD and asked Eng to confirm available telemetry fields.

2025-03-14 Marcus Patel (eng): Telemetry OK — can provide 90/95/99 latency, load, queue depth, and pod-level eviction rates. Suggested adding a synthetic 'stress forecast' signal from autoscaler logs.

2025-03-15 Design critique (team): Remove dense tables — replaced with 'resilience lanes' and horizontal sparkline stacks to improve scanability. Need accessible color tokens for severity levels (low/medium/high/critical).
2025-03-16 PM review (Liam O'Connor): Approved scope but requested clearer CTA for 'Request burst' to include owner and duration. Flagged cost visibility as required before approval flow.

2025-03-17 Usability testing (3 SREs): Users liked the orbit summary but wanted one-click drilldown to recent incidents. Added micro-interaction: orbit click opens incident timeline filtered to that pool.
source.acceptance_criteria: 1) Console displays per-pool health-orbit with latency, load, and eviction severity; 2) Autoscaling composer can simulate rule changes and show expected throughput/latency; 3) Burst request flow prompts for owner/duration and shows cost delta before submit; 4) Interaction spec delivered and engineering sign-off obtained; 5) Accessibility contrast and keyboard flows validated.
source.next_steps: Finalize high-fidelity screens and handoff tokens by 2025-03-19; schedule implementation sync with ENG on 2025-03-20; set up feature flag gating and Canary rollout plan with PM.
source.design_notes: Avoid red/green only indicators; use combined icon + color + text severity. 'Orbit' should be semantic: inner ring = immediate criticality, outer rings = trend windows (1h, 24h). Burst ledger modeled after financial ledger rows with editable approvals. Provide granular presets for common burst scenarios (short spike, scheduled batch, maintenance drain).
source.review_feedback: Marcus Patel: Good overall. Engineering ask: make orbit payload minimal — 12 fields max. Liam O'Connor: Add audit trail entry for each burst approval (user, time, duration, cost). Accessibility review: ensure orbit SVG aria-labels and focusable sectors.
source.risks: Telemetry gaps for older pools may show 'unknown' state; fallback UI must surface missing data gracefully. Burst approvals can be abused — product to design rate limits and RBAC scoping. Autoscaler simulation is approximate; must include 'confidence' band in UI.
source.outcomes: Expected outcomes are faster incident triage (reduce on-call resolution time by ~20%), clearer cost-preview for bursts, and fewer emergency scale-ups without documented approvals.
source.notes_for_engineering: Deliver three API endpoints: /health-orbit (summary), /autoscale-simulate (policy preview), /burst-request (create/approve/rollback). Provide example payloads in the Handoff doc in Figma file. Engineering to own final performance budget for orbit refresh rate (target: 5s for active pools).

source.title: Debug Composer: Incident Playbook & Payload Lenses (Prototype & Review)
source.creator: Maya Chen
source.description: Design a consolidated console surface called "Debug Composer" that combines an incident playbook, interactive payload lenses, and retry orchestration controls. The goal is to reduce mean time to triage (MTTT) for failed requests by putting contextualized debugging actions and structured guidance in one place. This ticket covers the high-fidelity prototype, annotated interaction specs, and a short internal review session with PM/Eng for alignment. Scope includes: failed request list -> composer panel, payload lenses (raw, delta, KV-cache timeline), retry orchestration bar, error taxonomy tagger, and cost/latency overlays tied to token usage. Scope excludes backend API changes (handled by ENG) and long-term storage/retention policies.
acceptance_criteria: Prototype screens exist for (1) composer collapsed/expanded states, (2) payload lens modes (raw/diff/kv-timeline), (3) retry orchestration flow, and (4) rollout-compatible model-variant dropdown.
Interaction spec documents keyboard and mouse flows for: open composer from failed-request row, attach a playbook step, run a single retry, and save a retry template.
Annotated handoff artifacts (Figma + Confluence) with component specs, spacing tokens, and accessibility notes (contrast, focus states) are published.
PM sign-off recorded and Eng agrees on API contracts to unblock implementation (list of contract endpoints in notes).
Usability checklist: 3 designers / 2 engineers walked through prototype and logged at least 6 qualitative observations; critical issues addressed or triaged into follow-ups.
notes: 2025-03-02 — Kickoff: Align w/ Diego Alvarez (PM) on goals: reduce MTTT for high-cost failed requests and make retry plumbing visible in Console. Agreed metrics: time-to-first-successful-retry, fraction of incidents resolved without infra-side rollback.
2025-03-05 — Initial concepts sketched: ‘sheet-style’ composer from failed-request row vs separate workspace. Chose sheet due to lower context switching per research sessions.

2025-03-07 — Design review w/ Sofia Iglesias (Eng): discussed feasibility of KV-cache timeline and payload delta diffs. Eng recommended incremental surface: start with client-side diffing (sha + compressed diff) and later add server-side enriched metadata (cache hits, kernel choices).

2025-03-09 — Prototype v1 created in Figma: https://redwood-fg.example.com/file/DebugComposer/v1 (internal). Includes: composer collapsed state, expanded lens with 3 tabs (Raw / Delta / KV-Timeline), retry bar, cost overlay toggle.

2025-03-11 — PM feedback: Add explicit error taxonomy tags (transient, quota, model-decode, infra) to help filter and trigger recommended playbook steps. Also requested a small callout for policy-related failures (sensitive content) with safe-fallback suggestions.

2025-03-12 — Research: Guerrilla usability with 6 engineers. Findings: (a) users expect retry to pre-populate params from original request, (b) cost overlay should surface per-token delta for each retry, (c) KV-timeline needs a compact mode for long sessions. Logged observations in Confluence: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/CONSOLE/Debug+Composer+Research

2025-03-13 — Design iteration: Introduced retry templates (save/reuse) and 'canary retry' toggle (route to staging model variant). Added visual affordance for model-variant rollouts: small badge showing variant and latency profile.

2025-03-14 — Engineering sync: ENG-48123 agreed to expose endpoints: /requests/{id}/payload, /requests/{id}/retries, /models/{variant}/preview-cost. ENG proposed a fallback plan: if model variant preview isn't available, show estimated cost using quantized token heuristics.

2025-03-15 — Internal review scheduled with PM, Eng, Security (15–30 min). Security asked for guidance on handling PII in payload viewer; decision: sanitize by default with a clear toggle to reveal raw payload after audit logging. Action: create security checklist for payload-surface features.
review_feedback: PM (Diego): Prioritize taxonomy-driven quick actions; add a recommended sequence the first time an error class is seen.
Eng (Sofia): Keep retry orchestration UI declarative; prefer buttons that emit events to backend rather than client-only state machine.
Security (Nora): Sanitize by default; require an audit log entry when a user reveals raw payload; block raw view for datasets marked 'restricted'.
Design peer (Asha Rao): Consider micro-animations for lens transitions to preserve mental model when switching tabs; ensure animations respect reduced-motion setting.
next_steps: Finalize accessibility annotations (contrast ratios, focus order) — owner: Ethan (due 2025-03-17)
Produce API contract doc with ENG-48123 & ENG-49100 — owner: Sofia (due 2025-03-18)
Run 2 moderated usability sessions with 4 platform engineers — owner: Priya (scheduled 2025-03-19)
Create security checklist and small secure-by-default UX copy for payload reveal — owner: Nora (due 2025-03-18)

source.title: Failure Polygraph: consolidated failure forensics & retry composer
source.creator: Maya Patel
source.description: Design a unified investigative surface inside Redwood Console that combines a failed-request timeline, payload inspection pane, retry composer, and error taxonomy lens. The goal is to reduce mean-time-to-resolution for production failures by giving engineers a single place to triage, compose deterministic retries, and attach rollout/rollback context. This prototype should also surface cost impact per failure class and link to rollout state so operators can correlate incidents with rollouts and quota changes.
acceptance_criteria: High-fidelity Figma screens for the Failure Polygraph flow: listing -> detail -> retry composer -> resolution notes
A working clickable prototype (Figma) demonstrating composing a retry with KV cache clearing, model variant fallback, and canary rollback suggestion
Design tokens & responsive spec for desktop and compact tablet layouts
Annotated handoff with edge-cases: partial payload redaction rules, GDPR hints, and SSO gating for payload export
Design review sign-off from Eng and PM and at least two user-testing sessions recorded with support engineers
design_notes: Primary navigation entry: Diagnostics -> Failure Polygraph
List view uses density buckets (failed, retried, resolved) and allows live-filtering by rollout id, model, and cost impact

Detail pane split into three columns: 1) request metadata + timeline; 2) payload inspector with redact/expand controls and structured diff; 3) retry composer with suggested mutations and cost delta preview
Error taxonomy engine surface: clickable cards that show sample failing payloads, mutation suggestions, and historical regression charts
Include a lightweight 'Cost Impact' ribbon that estimates token spend attributable to the failure cluster and shows per-retry projected cost
Design avoids exposing full PII: show redaction markers and allow controlled export only for SSO-admins
Provide graceful fallbacks for private deployments where KV cache flushing APIs are not available; composer should show guidance instead of live actions
comments: 2025-03-02 - Maya Patel: Initial sketches uploaded to Figma. Focused on making retry composer immediately actionable from a failed request card.
2025-03-04 - Rafael Ortega: PM feedback - emphasize rollout correlation earlier in the flow; add a small 'affects rollout' flag on list rows.
2025-03-06 - Lina Hofstad: Eng review - need to show when retry will hit cached KV state; suggested inline tooltip explaining KV-consistency windows.
2025-03-08 - User test (Support Engs): Observed confusion around permitted actions for private deployments; added note to show 'action availability' badges.
2025-03-10 - Ava Chen: Updated prototype with cost ribbon and three new mutation presets (strip-attachments, reduce-length, force-variant-B).
2025-03-12 - Rafael Ortega: Ready for design review; requested sample scripts for composing retries to be attached to the PR.
2025-03-12 - Maya Patel: Submitted design review doc in Confluence and pinged Eng and SRE for sign-off.
next_steps: Address Eng feedback about KV-consistency tooltip and include API contract snippets from ENG-4812
Run two 30-minute design walkthroughs with Support and Platform teams (target dates: 2025-03-14 and 2025-03-17)
Finalize Figma tokens and export component spec for the Console engineering team
Add a short handoff checklist in Confluence linking to rollout correlation dashboard and canary rollback playbook
Schedule security gating review if design changes introduce new export capabilities (tentative after engineering feasibility)

source.title: Incident Handoff Drawer and Postmortem Shortcuts for Alerts
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design a compact, persistent 'Handoff Drawer' that surfaces SLO context, on-call assignment, and one-click postmortem links within the Alerts timeline and alert setup flow. Goal: reduce friction during incident transfers and ensure runbooks/postmortem artifacts are discoverable from the alert UI. The drawer should work in both desktop and narrow-width tablet layouts and support keyboard navigation and screen readers.
source.background: On recent incidents, handoffs have relied on noisy Slack threads and manual linking to postmortems. Pager and alert noise leads to missed context (SLO burn, recent deploys). We want an in-console surface where the alert owner can attach or link a postmortem, see the last 3 related incidents, and trigger a handoff card that pins the next on-call with a timestamp and optional notes.
research_insights: Support engineers say 60% of handoff confusion comes from missing 'why' context (recent rollouts, SLO trend)
On-call swap happens 2-3x per long incident — quick access to postmortems reduces duplicated troubleshooting work
Users prefer contextual links over separate navigation; they want to remain in the timeline while opening artifacts
design_goals: Surface SLO status (72h trend + current burn) inline in alert header
Make adding postmortem links a 1-click action from the alert and handoff drawer
Provide a compact handoff card with suggested assignee, ETA, and short notes
Preserve console performance — drawer should lazy-load heavy attachments
Meet WCAG keyboard and screen reader requirements for dynamic content
acceptance_criteria: Handoff drawer can be opened from alert timeline and alert detail header
SLO widget shows percent of error budget used and 72h sparkline
Ability to attach an existing postmortem (link) and create a new postmortem placeholder
Handoff card can be created with assignee, ETA, and optional free-text notes
Accessibility: drawer focus trap, ARIA labels, and keyboard-only workflow verified
Telemetry: events emitted for drawer_open, handoff_create, postmortem_attach
tradeoffs_and_open_issues: Inlining SLO graphs may increase initial payload; decided to load SLO widget asynchronously
We considered auto-populating suggested assignee from rota but legal asked for opt-out — will add a toggle
Linking to external postmortems (Drive/Confluence) requires link validation and ACL checks — engineering to scope
Mobile layout: for very small viewports the drawer becomes a full-screen modal — needs QA
review_feedback: PM (Jordan Patel): Like the friction reduction, ask to add an explicit 'handoff accepted' button for auditing
Eng (Priya Nair): Prefer emitting structured telemetry and defer inline file previews to avoid S3 permission issues
Security (Liam O'Neill): Add a check for external link domains and store only canonical links, not file contents
Design (Marisol Vega): Tighten spacing around SLO sparkline for visual clarity at narrow widths
decisions: Async-load SLO widget to keep alert list snappy
Handoff cards are ephemeral objects attached to the alert timeline with audit entries
Postmortem links stored as canonical URLs; creation creates a placeholder page in Confluence via a webhook
comments: 2025-02-12 Ava Chen: Initial ticket created after sync with PM and oncall. Added prototype links.
2025-02-18 Rafael Ortega: Draft designs posted to Figma; requesting engineering feasibility review next week.
2025-02-24 Priya Nair: Feasibility OK; need API to validate link ACLs and endpoint for creating placeholder postmortems.
2025-02-28 Jordan Patel: Requested 'handoff accepted' audit event — added to acceptance criteria.
2025-03-04 Marisol Vega: Updated prototype with keyboard focus states and ARIA labels; ready for design review.
2025-03-06 Liam O'Neill: Security sign-off pending small domain allowlist change; filed ENG dependency ENG-6098.
next_steps: Address ENG-6098 (link ACL check) and ENG-6142 (telemetry schema) — blocking engineering tasks
Finalize QA checklist for keyboard flows and ARIA conformance
Schedule design review with support rotations team and SRE to validate SLO visualizations
Prepare rollout notes for gradual enablement (beta flag per org)

source.title: Token Spend Explorer — Role-aware Attribution & Pricing Presets
source.creator: Leah Park
source.description:
Goal: Design a Token Spend Explorer UX that gives billing owners and engineers a single-pane view to understand token-level spend across models, routes, and users, and to export cost-attribution results. The view must support role-aware defaults (Billing Admin vs Engineering Lead), pricing presets (flat-rate, slabed-tiers, custom per-model overrides), and an export wizard that produces a reconciliable CSV for accounting.

Scope:
- Main explorer surface: filter bar (time range, model, route, project tag, user role), column composer (tokens, prompt tokens, completion tokens, cost, cost-per-1k, cache-savings estimate), and grouped breakdown cards (by route, by model, by tag).
- Attribution modes: direct token attribution (by request), proportional attribution (split across route components), and cohort-level allocation (by project or customer tag).
- Pricing presets: built-in templates (Hosted-default, Dedicated-commit, Enterprise-custom) plus UI to create & apply a custom pricing profile that affects computed cost previews.
- Export wizard: stepper that selects attribution mode, pricing profile, columns, and reconciliation keys (request_id, session_id). Output: CSV and downloadable JSON lines for audit ingestion.
- Role-aware UI: Billing Admin sees pricing presets and export columns by default; Engineer sees latency/cost tradeoffs and sample prompts usage in addition to cost.

Design constraints and non-goals:
- Does not implement live cost syncing to billing system; export must be reconciliable but ingestion is a separate integration task.
- Does not include per-token content inspection; only metadata-driven attribution.

UX goals:
- Fast insight: median cost-per-call and 95th percentile token spikes at top of page.
- Explainability: tooltip-friendly equations showing how cost derived from tokens + pricing profile.
- Confidence for finance: export includes checksums and a provenance header with model version, pricing profile id, and time window.

Acceptance criteria:
- Prototype demonstrates switching attribution modes and shows immediate cost recalculation.
- Export wizard produces a sample CSV (<= 100 rows) for reviewer validation.
- Role-aware defaults applied and visible in header.
- Accessibility: all interactive elements keyboard-focusable and color contrast meets AA.

Design artifacts and links: [Figma token spend explorer prototype] https://www.figma.com/file/XXXX/Token-Spend-Explorer, [Design brief] https://docs.redwood.ai/confluence/token-cost-explorer, [Sample export spec] https://drive.redwood.ai/exports/token_export_spec_v1.csv
design_notes: Show cost delta when applying a pricing preset to help non-billing users see impact
Add provenance header and md5 checksum to exported file to satisfy finance audit needs
Use a column composer so power users can create compact exports without cluttering the main table
Cache-savings estimate should surface when prefix KV caching is enabled for a route
Provide an inline sample row that updates when filters change to increase trust
tradeoffs: Real-time recalculation vs UI latency: we debounce changes and show a 'stale' badge during heavy recalculation
Complex pricing expressions vs ease-of-use: provide both simple presets and an advanced expression editor behind an 'Advanced' toggle
Attribution fidelity vs privacy: cohort-level allocation avoids exposing per-user PII by default
Full audit ingestion pipeline is outside scope; CSV export is the first step to unblock finance
review_comments: Marco Ruiz (PM) 2025-02-25: Can we expose a quick toggle to apply 'Dedicated commit' pricing to a filtered set of models? Need for finance reconciliation.
Jordan Kim (Eng) 2025-02-27: Backend will need a pricing profile id and evaluation endpoint; prototype should include a sample API contract for review.
Sana Patel (Finance) 2025-03-01: Please include currency code and timezone in export header. Also request a checksum and row count field for each export.
Ava Chen (Design) 2025-03-03: Updated prototype to include provenance header and an example sample-row preview. Requesting accessibility review before sign-off.

title: Token Spend Explorer — Role-aware Attribution & Pricing Presets
preview:
Goal: Design a Token Spend Explorer UX that gives billing owners and engineers a single-pane view to understand token-level spend across models, routes, and users, and to export cost-attribution results. The view must support role-aware defaults (Billing Admin vs Engineering Lead), pricing presets (flat-rate, slabed-tiers, custom per-model overrides), and an export wizard that produces a reconciliable CSV for accounting.

Scope:
- Main explorer surface: filter bar (time range, model, route, project tag, user role), column composer (tokens, prompt tokens, completion tokens, cost, cost-per-1k, cache-savings estimate), and grouped breakdown cards (by route, by model, by tag).
- Attribution modes: direct token attribution (by request), proportional attribution (split across route components), and cohort-level allocation (by project or customer tag).
- Pricing presets: built-in templates (Hosted-default, Dedicated-commit, Enterprise-custom) plus UI to create & apply a custom pricing profile that affects computed cost previews.
- Export wizard: stepper that selects attribution mode, pricing profile, columns, and reconciliation keys (request_id, session_id). Output: CSV and downloadable JSON lines for audit ingestion.
- Role-aware UI: Billing Admin sees pricing presets and export columns by default; Engineer sees latency/cost tradeoffs and sample prompts usage in addition to cost.

Design constraints and non-goals:
- Does not implement live cost syncing to billing system; export must be reconciliable but ingestion is a separate integration task.
- Does not include per-token content inspection; only metadata-driven attribution.

UX goals:
- Fast insight: median cost-per-call and 95th percentile token spikes at top of page.
- Explainability: tooltip-friendly equations showing how cost derived from tokens + pricing profile.
- Confidence for finance: export includes checksums and a provenance header with model version, pricing profile id, and time window.

Acceptance criteria:
- Prototype demonstrates switching attribution modes and shows immediate cost recalculation.
- Export wizard produces a sample CSV (<= 100 rows) for reviewer validation.
- Role-aware defaults applied and visible in header.
- Accessibility: all interactive elements keyboard-focusable and color contrast meets AA.

Design artifacts and links: [Figma token spend explorer prototype] https://www.figma.com/file/XXXX/Token-Spend-Explorer, [Design brief] https://docs.redwood.ai/confluence/token-cost-explorer, [Sample export spec] https://drive.redwood.ai/exports/token_export_spec_v1.csv

source.title: Compact Alert Composer Canvas — Guided Rules, Handoff Shortcuts, and Cost Footprint Ribbon
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design a condensed Alert Composer canvas that surfaces three primary dimensions in a single, scannable strip: (1) guided rule creation (thresholds, noisy suppression heuristics), (2) on-call handoff quicklinks (postmortem templates, responder selection, runbook pinning), and (3) an inline cost footprint ribbon that estimates token/compute cost impact and suggests cost-savvy mitigation (aggregation window, sampling, dedup). The goal is to reduce time-to-alert creation for platform users, reduce noisy alerts by encouraging sensible defaults, and make cost tradeoffs explicit at creation time.

2025-02-18 — Low-fi sketches reviewed with Design Guild. Decided on progressive disclosure: compact row -> expanded modal on 'advanced' click. Removed multi-step wizard approach to keep flow fast for SLAs-level alerts.

2025-02-25 — Mid-fi prototype uploaded to Figma. Usability session with 6 SREs showed faster task completion vs current composer (median 24s -> 11s). Pain: unclear semantics of 'deduplicate by trace id' toggle; added microcopy and help tooltip.
2025-03-01 — Design review with Eng: agreed to implement cost estimate as a best-effort (not blocking create) using a new endpoint (ENG-61233). PR referenced for shared components: GH #1298.
2025-03-04 — PM review: requested adding postmortem link auto-populate when 'notify on-call' is enabled. Added as acceptance criterion. Preparing Figma handoff tokens and redlines.

user_research: 6 SRE interviews (internal) — tasks: create alert for high-latency endpoint; create alert for error spike grouped by service
Findings: users prefer single-line summaries, want quick toggle for 'escalate to phone', fear false-positives from naive thresholds
Recommendation: on-by-default sensible thresholds with lightweight 'simulate' preview
decisions: Use compact single-row composer by default with chevron to expand to advanced options
Show inline cost footprint as three buckets: 'per-incident compute', 'per-hour sampling', 'expected monthly impact' (estimates only)
Expose two handoff quicklinks: 'Create postmortem draft' and 'Pin runbook' (deep-links to Postmortem generator and Runbook manager)
Keep alert creation non-blocking if cost estimate endpoint is slow — fallback to 'estimate unavailable' and banner guidance

Cost ribbon displays estimate or graceful fallback within 500ms for 90% of requests (backend SLA handled by ENG-61233).
Handoff quicklinks open respective flows prefilled with alert metadata (service, filter, timestamp).
Tooltips and microcopy added for dedupe and sampling toggles; verified in usability test with 6 participants.
Accessibility: ribbon meets AA contrast and quicklinks reachable via keyboard (tab order).

Schedule design-qa walkthrough during implementation (Liam + Ava + Maya)

source.title: Contributor stewardship, deprecation & retirement playbook for design system
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Establish a single, pragmatic playbook for accepting contributions to the design system, lifecycle review for components and tokens, and a safe deprecation/retirement process. This ticket consolidates prior ad-hoc guidance into a playbook that: 1) makes contribution expectations clear, 2) defines an RFC + review cadence for breaking changes, 3) defines telemetry and usage thresholds for safe retirement, and 4) gives migration guidance for tokens and components. Goal: reduce friction for contributors while making risk visible to product and infra stakeholders.
acceptance_criteria: A written contribution checklist published in the repo and linked from the design system docs
RFC template and decision timeline documented and linked to the PR flow
Deprecation matrix with usage thresholds and required steps for retirement
Migration guide outline for token renames and variant merges
Stakeholder sign-off from Eng Infra, Product, and Security (if applicable)
review_checklist: Design: visual spec, responsive rules, accessibility notes (WCAG 2.1 AA), Figma file link
Engineering: implementation API surface, props compatibility, performance considerations (render cost, bundle size), test coverage
Docs: usage examples, migration snippet for vN -> vN+1
Telemetry: metrics to add (usage count, broken usages, error rates) and how to collect
Product: customer impact assessment and known users list
Security/Privacy: data handling notes; mark if review required
Rollout: suggested canary percentage, fallback behaviour, rollback steps
rfc_process: When a change is potentially breaking (API, token name, behavior): author opens an RFC doc in the design repo under /rfc with template populated
RFC must include: motivation, non-goals, alternatives considered, migration burden estimate, owner, and suggested timeline
Review period: 10 business days for standard changes, 20 business days for high-impact changes
Decisions recorded using the RFC status tags: Proposed -> In Review -> Approved -> Scheduled -> Deprecated
An RFC reaching Approved must include a rollout plan and required telemetry signals to validate success
If RFC impacts infra/hosting costs (ENG label), add ENG owner approval before Scheduling
source.deprecation_policy_summary: Deprecation should be explicit, timeboxed, and observable. No component or token is removed without a public deprecation notice, migration guidance, and telemetry showing low usage or a valid business case for removal.
deprecation_matrix: Soft deprecation (alias): provide backward-compatible alias; announce in changelog; no removal
Standard deprecation: mark as deprecated in docs and code, add console warnings, schedule removal in 3 months with migration guide
High-impact deprecation: for items used by >1% active projects or core infra; require migration tooling and 6+ month window with stakeholder outreach
Immediate removal: only for security or legal reasons; document reason and notify all consumers; implement emergency rollback plan
migration_strategy: Tokens: prefer additive token introduction, deprecate old tokens via aliases that emit dev-time warnings
Components: maintain prop-level compat shims for one major cycle; provide codemods for JS/TS consumers
Charts/tables: provide data shape adapters and examples for migrating from old props to new props
Automated tooling: codemods, linters (eslint rules) and a migration guide in the repo root
Fallback: design tokens support fallback values in CSS Custom Properties for gradual rollout
rollout_playbook: Pre-release: smoke tests, visual regression snapshots, perf bench (render time, bundle impact)
Canary: roll change to 5–10% of product routes or internal apps for 72 hours
Observe: collect telemetry (usage delta, errors, UI regressions) and run accessibility scans
Scale: increase to 50% for 1 week if metrics stable
Full release: ship to all with clear migration docs and changelog entry
Retirement reminder: automated reminders to owners at 4 weeks and 1 week before removal
tasks: Draft contribution checklist and add to CONTRIBUTING.md
Create RFC template and add /rfc folder in design repo
Add ESLint rule for deprecated token usage detection
Prototype codemod for primary token rename path
Define telemetry events and add to telemetry backlog (ENG-2345)
Write migration guide for Button -> ButtonV2 and Tokens V1->V2
comments: 2025-02-13 - Ava Chen: Initial draft posted. Focus areas: tokens, buttons, tables/charts migration. Requested ENG lead (Rafael) to validate feasibility and perf concerns.
2025-02-18 - Rafael Gomez: Reviewed; added perf checklist items and asked to quantify bundle impact threshold. Flagged dependency on ENG-2345 for telemetry events.
2025-02-25 - Priya Nair (PM): Suggested adding a customer outreach step for high-impact deprecations and tying releases to sales/customer success notifications.
2025-02-28 - Design review session notes: consensus to use 3-month standard window for most deprecations, 6 months for high-impact. Agree to codemod approach for token renames.
2025-03-03 - Rafael Gomez: Attached prototype eslint rule PR: github.com/redwood/design-system/pull/462. Needs review from Eng infra and release owners.
2025-03-04 - Ava Chen: Updated doc to include rollout playbook and acceptance criteria. Ready for final review by PM and Eng leadership.

source.title: Semantic roles & focus management sweep for components: buttons, tables, charts, tokens
source.creator: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Goal: Complete a cross-component accessibility sweep focused on semantic roles, keyboard focus/order, skip/fallback behaviors, and contrast tokens for buttons, table controls, and data-visualization components so the design system is ready for the upcoming VPAT refresh and runtime consumers. This ticket covers design decisions, token adjustments, keyboard nav matrices, ARIA role recommendations, and a rollout plan with dev/QA acceptance criteria. Scope intentionally excludes content authoring flows (rich text editor) which are covered in another ticket.
acceptance_criteria: All buttons (primary/secondary/ghost/icon) have consistent focus styles using a single focus token and meet 3:1 contrast for the focus indicator against adjacent backgrounds
Table rows expose explicit role=row and row header mapping; keyboard up/down moves cell focus in a predictable order; sortable header buttons have aria-sort and aria-labels documented

Chart interactive elements (legend items, data points used as tooltips) expose proper roles (button/checkbox) where appropriate and have keyboard focus + visible focus ring that meets color contrast guidance
Token mapping document produced: old token -> new a11y-safe token with rationale and a migration guide for devs
Design spec and component guidance linked in Figma + Confluence and referenced in two implementation PRs
VPAT draft updated with changes and references to component-level test cases
tasks: Run final contrast checks across light/dark themes and produce table of failing combinations
Prototype unified focus token in Figma and create 3 variants (high-contrast, subtle, and dense) for engineering review
Document keyboard nav matrices for tables and charts (including edge cases for merged cells and virtualized tables)
Add aria roles and example markup snippets to component docs for buttons, tables, chart legends, and interactive marks
Work with Engineering to land changes in the tokens package and update Storybook stories with accessibility knobs
Coordinate with QA to add automated axe checks for all Storybook stories and add manual test cases for VPAT evidence
qa_plan: Automated: Add axe-core checks to CI for components touched by this ticket
Manual: QA checklist in Confluence with steps to validate focus order, aria attributes, keyboard shortcuts, and contrast on macOS Safari, Chrome, and Windows Edge
Accessibility engineer to run keyboard-only navigation against Storybook examples and attach video evidence for VPAT
comments: 2025-02-18 Priya Natarajan: Initial audit attached (Confluence) — major issues: inconsistent focus tokens, multiple custom roles on chart legend items, and table header buttons missing aria-sort states.
2025-02-21 Liam O'Connor: Mapped proposed token replacements and pushed a Figma prototype. Decided to normalize on a single outline focus token + optional inset shadow for dense lists.
2025-02-27 UX Review (Jana Müller): Recommended stronger focus contrast in dark theme; suggested we avoid using color alone to indicate sort state (add icon + aria-sort text).
2025-03-01 Eng WIP (Aisha Rahman): Token change PR opened (see dev link). Implemented keyboard nav per design for tables; need to handle virtualization edge cases — blocked for follow-up sub-issue.

2025-03-04 Accessibility Review (Oliver Chen): Quick pass on Storybook examples — focus ring passes contrast but a11y tests flagged missing role on chart legend items when rendered as plain text. Added to tasks.
