source.title: Adaptive Pool Tuner: Predictive Cost & Latency Tradeoff Canvas
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Operators of dedicated GPU pools need a fast way to tune autoscale and burst settings while understanding the expected cost and latency tradeoffs for the observed workload. Current pool controls are distributed across multiple panels; users must toggle between health, scaling policy, and cost dashboards to make a single configuration change. This leads to conservative settings, overprovisioning, and surprise bills.

Goal: Design a single, compact canvas — the Adaptive Pool Tuner — that surfaces predicted tail latency, median latency, and incremental cost for proposed autoscale and burst rule changes. The canvas should support short iterative workflows: propose change, simulate effect on a chosen workload profile, preview cost delta and SLO impact, save as draft or apply with rollout policy.

Scope:
- A compact left rail with workload selector (recent routes, custom trace upload).
- Central visualization showing current vs. simulated latency percentiles and throughput at configurable time slices.
- Right rail cost summary with per-hour and per-24h delta, and suggestions (e.g., reduce max-burst, increase cold-scale delay).
- Inline canary rollout controls: percent, duration, automatic rollback threshold.
- Export links to runbook and annotated snapshot for on-call.

Out of scope for this ticket: backend simulation engine; we will use a mocked predictive API for prototype. Deeper cost-model accuracy and multi-pool orchestration will be follow-ups.

Success metrics:
- Reduce number of console pages visited per tuning session to 1 (tracked via analytics).
- 30% fewer manual scaling overrides per pool in the first 90 days after rollout (pilot customers).
- Time-to-decision for ops engineers < 3 minutes for common tuning tasks.

Design constraints & considerations:
- Must be usable on 1280px width with collapsed left nav (compact mode).
- Accessibility: color contrast for visual bands, keyboard navigable controls, aria labels for simulation chart nodes.
- Mobile: view-only mode for on-call responders (not a full edit flow).
- Trust: show confidence band in simulations and a short note when predictions rely on sampled traces.

Deliverables:
- Low-fidelity flows (edge cases: high-variance workloads, sudden spike simulation).
- High-fidelity Figma prototype with interactive simulation slider and rollout config.
- Design spec (tokens, spacing, microcopy) and annotated accessibility checklist.
- Usability test plan for 6 internal operators + 2 pilot customers.

Links to artifacts: Figma: https://www.figma.com/file/EXAMPLE/adaptive-pool-tuner (prototype v1) | Confluence spec: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INF/Adaptive+Pool+Tuner | Mock API for sim: https://github.com/redwood-inference/pool-sim-mock/pull/14
acceptance_criteria: Prototype demonstrates a full flow from selecting workload to applying a canary rollout (mock backend).
Simulation chart displays current vs. proposed latency percentiles and a visible confidence band.
Cost summary shows delta in both absolute ($) and relative (%) terms and maps to billing SKU names.
Rollout controls include percent, duration, and an automatic rollback toggle with threshold input.
Design spec includes accessibility annotations and responsive behavior for compact screens.
activity: 2025-02-16 - Ava Chen: Initial brief and low-fi sketches uploaded to Confluence. Asked Eng to provide simulated payload shapes.
2025-02-18 - Marcus Patel (Eng): Provided sample workload traces and suggested adding an 'unexpected spike' toggle to the simulator.
2025-02-22 - Lena Morales (Design): High-fidelity prototype v0.2 created in Figma; included compact mode and keyboard flows. Shared with PM and frontend.
2025-02-25 - PM Review (Sofia Grant): Requested per-token cost mapping to billing SKU names and a clearer confidence note in the chart.
2025-02-27 - Frontend (Diego Alvarez): Feasibility check: interactive percentile slider will require new chart component; estimated 3 dev days for an initial implementation.
2025-03-01 - Usability test plan posted; participants: 4 infra engineers + 2 pilot operators. Goal: validate time-to-decision and comprehension of cost delta.

2025-03-03 - Design review meeting notes: small wording changes to rollout copy; agreed to surface 'estimated rollback time' when auto-rollback is enabled. Marked ready for engineering ticket creation.
next_steps: Create ENG ticket for interactive chart component (include sample payload and mocked endpoints).
Hand off Figma spec and annotatations to frontend with priority flags for accessibility items.
Run two moderated usability sessions by 2025-03-07 and synthesize findings for a quick iteration.
Coordinate with billing to map simulation outputs to SKU-level pricing for the cost delta.

The canvas should support short iterative workflows: propose change, simulate effect on a chosen workload profile, preview cost delta and SLO impact, save as draft or apply with rollout policy.

Scope:
- A compact left rail with workload selector (recent routes, custom trace upload).
- Central visualization showing current vs. simulated latency percentiles and throughput at configurable time slices.
- Right rail cost summary with per-hour and per-24h delta, and suggestions (e.g., reduce max-burst, increase cold-scale delay).
- Inline canary rollout controls: percent, duration, automatic rollback threshold.
- Export links to runbook and annotated snapshot for on-call.

Out of scope for this ticket: backend simulation engine; we will use a mocked predictive API for prototype. Deeper cost-model accuracy and multi-pool orchestration will be follow-ups.

Success metrics:
- Reduce number of console pages visited per tuning session to 1 (tracked via analytics).
- 30% fewer manual scaling overrides per pool in the first 90 days after rollout (pilot customers).
- Time-to-decision for ops engineers < 3 minutes for common tuning tasks.

Design constraints & considerations:
- Must be usable on 1280px width with collapsed left nav (compact mode).
- Accessibility: color contrast for visual bands, keyboard navigable controls, aria labels for simulation chart nodes.
- Mobile: view-only mode for on-call responders (not a full edit flow).
- Trust: show confidence band in simulations and a short note when predictions rely on sampled traces.

Deliverables:
- Low-fidelity flows (edge cases: high-variance workloads, sudden spike simulation).
- High-fidelity Figma prototype with interactive simulation slider and rollout config.
- Design spec (tokens, spacing, microcopy) and annotated accessibility checklist.
- Usability test plan for 6 internal operators + 2 pilot customers.

Links to artifacts: Figma: https://www.figma.com/file/EXAMPLE/adaptive-pool-tuner (prototype v1) | Confluence spec: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INF/Adaptive+Pool+Tuner | Mock API for sim: https://github.com/redwood-inference/pool-sim-mock/pull/14

source.title: Mini-carousel quickstart: compact hosted-API screenshots + code-toggle
source.creator: Maya Chen
source.description:
Goal: produce a compact, mobile-first set of Hosted API getting-started screenshots for the Quickstart micro-tour. Asset set should demonstrate: model selector microflow, inline request builder (prompt presets + param sliders), code-snippet toggle (Python/Node/cURL), token-cost preview overlay, and accessibility alt-text examples. Deliverables will be a carousel of 6 hero screens (desktop + mobile variants), export-ready PNGs and an associated code snippet text bundle for docs. Focus on clarity for first-time engineers and product-led sign-up flow.

Key design constraints: compact layout that fits 640x880 (mobile) and 1280x720 (web) hero slots; code snippet lines limited to 8 for thumbnails; visible cost-estimate pill (tokens/$) in top-right; accessible color contrast >= 4.5:1; keyboard focus states shown for model selector.

Acceptance criteria:
- 6 unique frames covering: (1) Welcome + model picker open, (2) Model details overlay with capability badges, (3) Request builder showing prompt presets and system message toggle, (4) Param sliders (max_tokens, temperature, top_p) + token-cost preview, (5) Code toggle showing Python snippet, and (6) Code toggle showing Node + cURL compact variants.
- Each frame: desktop and mobile exports, named with convention: hosted_quick_XXX_{desktop|mobile}.png.
- Designer notes and alt-text for each image included in a single text file for docs teams.
- PR created with assets + snippet bundle; screenshots validated in Staging docs site.

Non-goals: full-page console walkthroughs, deep dive into Dedicated or Private flows.

source.title: Signal Flow Navigator — lineage and lightweight request exploration
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Engineers need a fast, low-friction way to explore request lineage across services without the cognitive overhead of a full-forensics trace workspace. Current consoles focus on dense timelines and span-level forensic tools; customers asked for a lightweight explorer that surfaces end-to-end causality, correlation IDs, and cost/latency overlays for quick debugging and rollout validation.

Goal: Design a compact, keyboard-first 'Signal Flow Navigator' that lets users: 1) search by correlation ID or route, 2) see a condensed lineage graph + linear time-strip, 3) toggle span-grouping and sampling, and 4) overlay per-request cost and latency heatmarks. It must integrate with rollouts so PM/SEs can validate canaries and quickly escalate or rollback.

Scope: UX surface + clickable prototype (Figma) for console team review; include acceptance criteria, accessibility considerations, telemetry events to track, and handoff notes for frontend engineering. Not in scope: backend sampling pipeline changes (will be tracked as ENG deps).
acceptance_criteria: Search accepts correlation ID, request ID, or route and returns matching sessions within 2s in staging demo
Condensed lineage visualization shows service nodes and directional edges with hover-to-expand span summary
Linear time-strip supports pinch/zoom and keyboard left/right to move between spans and request boundaries
Cost and latency overlays toggle on/off; cost per request computed from existing billing estimator and matches dashboard within 5%
Rollout panel link from a selected request opens rollout details and shows whether request hit canary vs stable
Prototype has annotated telemetry events and at least 3 user-testing scenarios documented
comments: 2025-02-18 - Marcus Patel: Initial concept review — remove heavy swimlane headers to reduce noise; add keyboard navigation notes.
2025-02-20 - Priya Nair (PM): Need a clear path to show whether a request hit canary vs stable. Add a small badge on spans that originated from canary nodes.
2025-02-28 - Jose Alvarez (Eng): Backend will expose a lightweight lineage summary API (ENG-42001). We need field-level contract for node_id, parent_id, latency_ms, cost_estimate_cents.
2025-03-02 - Accessibility review by Leila Morris: Ensure color-only heatmarks have patterns and provide textual list view for screen readers.
updates: 2025-02-16 - Drafted low-fi sketches and flow diagrams; confirmed search semantics with PMs; started Figma file.
2025-02-21 - High-fi prototype v1 completed: added hover cards, zoomable time-strip, and cost overlay example.
2025-02-25 - Ran 6 moderated sessions with internal SRE and solutions engineers; captured 12 actionable insights (see User Testing Notes link).
2025-03-01 - Incorporated canary badge and added rollout quick-actions; prepared handoff checklist for frontend.
2025-03-03 - Submitted this ticket for design review; requested ENG dependency tracking under ENG-42001 and ENG-42078 (sampling flags).
source.decisions: Go-forward: Build the condensed lineage + linear time-strip hybrid (not a full waterfall). Default to aggregated span groups with an expand control for deep inspection. Cost overlay is opt-in and derives from existing billing estimator. Rollout integration will be a contextual panel with quick rollback link to the rollout UI. Keyboard navigation and compact list view are required for power users.
source.tradeoffs: Chose compact hybrid over full waterfall to lower onboarding friction and speed up common investigations; this reduces immediate visibility into every span detail, so we provide an expand mechanism to jump into the full trace-forensics workspace when needed. Cost estimator relies on eventual consistency of billing data — may show slight delta during live canary ramps.
next_steps: Finalize Figma annotations and export component spec (Ava)
Coordinate ENG contract for lineage-summary API and sampling flags (Marcus) - link ENG-42001, ENG-42078
Create a lightweight demo dataset for the staging env and coordinate QA scenarios (PM)
Add telemetry events to spec and validate privacy implications with Security (Leila)

source.title: Token Waterfall Slate: compact attribution + export patterns
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design and prototype a compact, high-density "Waterfall Slate" for the Redwood Console cost dashboard that surfaces token-level attribution (per-route, per-model, per-invocation) and supports multi-format export. The slate should present a layered token-cost waterfall (origin layers: prompt, completion, KV cache hit, prefix reuse, retries), allow quick pivoting by dimension (route, workspace, model family, quantization profile), and provide guided pricing-simulation controls (unit rate overrides, slab presets, reserved capacity adjustments). The goal is a single-pane summary that is readable for both engineers and finance stakeholders, with progressive disclosure for forensic detail and an export format suited for downstream chargeback and BI ingestion.
acceptance_criteria: Waterfall Slate mock exists in Figma with desktop and narrow-tablet variants
Ability to pivot primary breakdown by: route, model, workspace in the UI mock
Export produces both CSV (flattened rows) and NDJSON (per-invocation) artifacts with documented schema
Pricing simulation modal allows changing per-token unit and applying 3 slab presets; UI validates changes and shows delta preview
Design has accessibility annotations (color contrast, keyboard affordances) and a performance note for virtualization when >50k rows
design_notes: Use a stacked waterfall visualization where each band represents an attributable token bucket (prompt, completion, cache_hit, prefix_reuse, system_tokens, retries)
Compact row design: 3-line density for route rows, small-sparkline for trend, and a right-aligned cost micro-spark that expands on hover
Progressive disclosure: summary row shows aggregated cost and token count; clicking expands to invocation-level pane with filters and timeline scrubber
Export UX: primary CTA 'Export' opens a modal with format toggle (CSV | NDJSON | Pivot-CSV). Include pre-check showing estimated file size and retention TTL options
Pricing simulation: inline editable unit rate with immediate recalculation on the visible slice only (so users can compare scenarios without recomputing entire dataset)
Security: exported files respect workspace RBAC and export audit-events must be emitted to the platform audit log
decisions: Default breakdown = route + model_version; users can add secondary pivot dimensions via a pill selector

CSV schema chosen to be flattened: columns = timestamp, invocation_id, route, model, model_version, tokens_prompt, tokens_completion, tokens_cache_hit, cost_prompt, cost_completion, cost_cache, effective_unit_rate, workspace_id
NDJSON export will emit one JSON object per invocation to preserve nested attribution arrays and ease downstream ingestion
Keep 'compact' slate as default on dashboard to reduce vertical space consumption; detailed invocation panel available as a drill-in
Prefer client-side aggregation preview for small slices (<5k rows) and server-side aggregation for larger slices to avoid client OOM
tradeoffs: Detail vs density: showing invocation-level details inline increases cognitive load; we chose drill-in to balance readability and forensic capability
Export fidelity vs compute cost: NDJSON preserves full attribution but can be large; provide sampling and pre-aggregation options to limit size

Interactivity vs reproducibility: allowing live unit-rate edits is powerful for simulation but may confuse billing-safe defaults; we will surface a clear 'simulation' banner and not change persisted billing data
Latency vs freshness: computing exact per-invocation attribution for long ranges is expensive; default time window set to 7 days with options for on-demand full-range recompute
review_comments: Liam Rogers (PM): 'Love the compact slate. Please make it clear that exports are subject to workspace export quotas and show estimated row count before export.'
Priya Singh (Eng): 'Server-side aggregation approach seems fine, but we need a feature flag and metrics to monitor extra CPU cost when users trigger full-range NDJSON exports.'
Carlos Mendez (Data): 'NDJSON is good for ingestion. Add a schema version field and a hash of the export parameters so downstream ETL can detect schema drift.'
Zoe Martinez (Design): 'Contrast on the small-sparkline bars is low at small widths; tweak color ramp and add an optional monochrome outline.'
next_steps: Finalize Figma mock addressing Zoe's contrast feedback (owner: Maya) - due 2025-03-05
Product review with Liam and Carlos to lock CSV/NDJSON schema and retention defaults - meeting 2025-03-03
Spike for server cost estimation of NDJSON exports (ENG) - assign to Priya - estimate 3d
Create frontend PR implementing compact slate shell with mock data (Console repo) - follow PR #4821
Add export audit event instrumentation to platform audit service - add ticket in ENG backlog
ux_tests: Moderated test with 6 engineers and 3 finance partners focusing on pivot flows and export mental model
A/B test in Canary with 'compact slate' vs 'detail-first' variant to measure discovery and export rate
Performance test: open slate with 100k synthetic invocations to validate virtualization strategy

source.title: Adaptive FlowCards and Responsive Token Synthesis for Rollout Console
source.creator: Priya Shah
source.description: Objective: Create a single FlowCard component family and a responsive token synthesis approach that adapts density, contrast, and motion across rollouts dashboards. The goal is to reduce component sprawl in the Console, enable consistent canary/A-B rollout UIs, and make token-driven behavior (spacing, elevation, animation duration) predictable for engineering handoff. This ticket drives a Figma library delivery, accessibility pass, and a small prototype to validate interaction under live data loads.
source.problem_statement: The Rollouts and Cost views currently use multiple ad-hoc card components with varying spacing, focus states, and token mappings. This causes inconsistent user scanning patterns and extra effort for runtime styling variants. We need a unified, constraint-driven card system that adapts to density (compact, default, spacious), supports feature flags for rollout state, and maps clearly to runtime tokens for simple implementation by the frontend team.
acceptance_criteria: Figma library contains 3 density variants (compact, default, spacious) for FlowCard with documented token mappings
Interaction spec includes keyboard focus, hover, and disabled/unstable rollout states with accessible contrast ratios
Design tokens file (export-ready) enumerates spacing, elevation, radii, and motion tokens used by cards
Prototype (iframe embed) demonstrates cards in a mocked rollout list with 1000 items using virtualization styling hints
Frontend engineering has PR checklist and two sample components that consume the token set
decisions: Adopt constraint-based variants instead of fully separate components to reduce variant explosion
Expose density as a single top-level token (card.density) that resolves spacing, typography scale, and elevation
Use neutral-foreground semantic tokens for pill text to avoid per-page overrides
Prefer moderate motion by default; reduce motion on reduced-motion UA preference
Prototype will use a simplified token JSON for engineering consumption (no nested objects)
tradeoffs: Constraint-based approach reduces duplication but requires stricter documentation to avoid misuse
One density token simplifies runtime API but may require a brief migration for existing surfaces
Keeping motion tokens coarse avoids huge token proliferation but loses some micro-optimization flexibility
progress_updates: 2025-01-20 Priya Shah: Kickoff — scoped deliverables and set milestones. Shared initial PRD link to the Console design program drive.
2025-01-27 Ava Chen: Completed first-pass token matrix for spacing and elevation. Ran contrast checks against current color tokens; two pill states failed AA for small text.
2025-02-03 Design Review: Team accepted density token model but requested accessible focus ring adjustments and clarified hover behavior on disabled items.
2025-02-11 Engineering Sync (Mateo Ruiz): Confirmed feasibility of a single density prop; asked for explicit token exports and example CSS variables.
2025-02-18 Prototype: Lightweight storybook build demonstrating FlowCard variants. Performance note: virtualization padding hints added to reduce layout thrash.
2025-02-25 Accessibility Pass (Ava Chen): Updated pill contrast to pass AA and added guidance for icon-only actions to include labels for screen readers.
2025-02-28 QA Handoff: Prepared component spec and exported tokens JSON; awaiting ENG PR and design acceptance checklist completion.
review_feedback: PM (Liam O'Connor): Likes unified token approach; asked for clearer error states for rollout failures
Eng (Mateo Ruiz): Requested example migration doc for existing cards and a minimal set of CSS variable names
Security (Nina Patel): No security impact but requested note to avoid shipping SSO modal inside FlowCard bodies
Design Ops (Maya Gonzalez): Suggested naming convention for tokens to match Console taxonomy (card.* vs surface.*)
next_steps: Finalize token JSON and export naming conventions by 2025-03-04
Create migration guide and small dashboard demo for ENG to validate runtime wiring
Run a user session with Solutions team to observe scanning patterns across density variants
Merge initial ENG PR behind feature flag and run canary on staging rollouts view
notes: Keep variable names flat to simplify consumption in both CSS and runtime theming (no nested token objects).
When adding new status pills, update the token mapping doc and add accessibility color checks as part of the PR template.
Aim for no more than 3 elevation levels for cards to keep visual language coherent across Console surfaces.

source.description: Produce a focused set of UX assets and high-fidelity screenshots for the Feb catalog release notes: primary deliverables are a pricing matrix visualization (desktop + mobile + dark mode), model variant rows (standard/quantized/fast), deprecation banners and the migration quickstart modal. These will be used in the public release notes, the Console release banner, and the onboarding quickstart flow that surfaces newly added or deprecated models. The designs must be consistent with the refreshed catalog visual language and include explicit cost/tooling callouts (per-token and estimated hour cost), region availability badges, and an accessible deprecation CTA flow.

Screenshots that match the production Console markup (font size, spacing within 8px grid)

Pricing matrix shows per-model tier rows and an expanded row with cost breakdown tooltip

Catalog: per-model info card with region availability badges (us-west, eu-central, apac) and capability icons

source.title: Dark-mode interaction ink reflow and graph legend hierarchy
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Problem: In deep-dark console themes interactive elements (buttons, menus, toggles) exhibit visual artifacts and perceived weight due to 'ink' reflow and overlay rules. Chart legends and color keys lose hierarchy against near-black surfaces, and dense tables depend on thin separators that drop below readable contrast at small sizes. Scope: Create a token mapping and component override proposal for deep/dense dark surfaces that preserves accessible contrast, preserves brand accent identity, and avoids visual clutter across tables, charts, and controls. Deliverables: 1) Token transform rules (alpha overlays, elevation-strength multipliers, accent-saturation clamp) 2) Component override spec for Button / Menu / Toggle / Table row / Chart legend 3) Figma tokens and component variants 4) Implementation guidance for theme engine (runtime mapping + opt-in canary) 5) Automated contrast tests and visual regression sets.
source.acceptance_criteria: 1) New dark token set (overlay-strength, interactive-ink-pressed, accent-legend-contrast, table-separator-visibility) exists in Figma and tokens.json; 2) Component overrides defined with clear fallbacks and examples for three density settings (compact, default, dense); 3) All interactive states meet WCAG AA contrast against their immediate surface for body text and icons; 4) Chart legend examples (categorical and continuous) show preserved brand hue with increased perceived contrast without exceeding saturation clamp; 5) Implementation PR (console-theme) includes unit/storybook examples and a runtime toggle; 6) Canary rollout metrics show no >1% regression in user task success on navigation and no increase in error reports in 72h after rollout.
comments: 2025-02-10 Ava Chen: Initial triage and problem statement added. Observed issues on staging with 'midnight' theme—buttons appear visually heavier on hover due to high overlay alpha. Logged sample screenshots in Figma.

2025-02-12 Diego Morales: Did a quick audit of current token stack. Root cause is a flat conversion from light tokens to dark tokens (invert + mute) causing inconsistent elevation perception. Proposed a layered overlay approach. Assigned to design to spec tokens.
2025-02-14 PM-review (Jordan Patel): Agrees with risk profile; asked to include A/B test plan for canary and to define metric targets for legend readability (time-to-identify color key).

2025-02-16 Design review (Ava/Diego/Celeste): Ran three Figma variants. Decided on overlay-strength=0.18 for primary surfaces and interactive-ink-pressed alpha=0.28 for pressed states. Trade-off: slightly higher perceived ink vs. potential brand accent shift—mitigate with saturation clamp and brand-safe hue remapping.

2025-02-18 Engineering feedback (Liam R.): Runtime mapping is feasible. Suggested adding a feature-flag to enable legacy behavior for Dedicated customers during rollout. Also requested token names be aligned with existing runtime keys (interactive.ink.pressed, overlay.strength, accent.legend.contrast).
2025-02-19 QA (Maya): Started building contrast test harness. Identified two chart palettes where low-saturation hues fail; created remediation palette and added to attachments.

source.title: Incident-aware Upgrade Schedule Editor for Private Clusters
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Operators of private/VPC clusters need a single, actionable place in the Console to schedule cluster upgrades while taking active incidents, maintenance windows, and customer SLOs into account. Current upgrade scheduling is too static: it doesn't show ongoing incidents, doesn't suggest safe canary windows, and lacks a clear rollback/abort affordance for mid-upgrade incidents.

Goal: Design an interactive Upgrade Schedule Editor that: 1) surfaces current incident state and cluster health when picking times; 2) suggests safe windows based on SLA/SLO and past busy-hours telemetry; 3) supports canary waves, staged rollouts, and quick abort/rollback actions; 4) integrates RBAC so only authorized personas can change critical windows; and 5) provides clear audit trail + links to incident runbooks.

Scope of this ticket: UX prototypes and validation for the Editor (desktop & compact tablet widths), microcopy for alerts/warnings, edge-case flows (ongoing incident, multiple clusters in a pool, cross-region dependencies), and final handoff assets for engineering. Not in scope: backend scheduling engine work, feature flagging logic, or on-prem installer changes (those are separate ENG tickets).

Key user stories:
- As an SRE, I want the editor to warn me if an active incident affects >10% of nodes so I can delay the upgrade.
- As a Platform PM, I want suggested canary windows that minimize customer impact based on historical peak hours.
- As an Operator, I want a single-click abort that stops rollout and triggers rollback playbooks.
- As an Admin, I want audit metadata (who scheduled, what changed) surfaced in the timeline and exportable as CSV.
acceptance_criteria: Prototype includes a timeline view with incident overlay and selectable time windows
Editor supports staged canary waves with configurable percentages and ordering
Warnings are shown when selected windows overlap active incidents or known maintenance blocks
RBAC gates are reflected in the UI (scheduling disabled for read-only users) and shown in mockups
Accessible keyboard interactions for timeline selection and modal dialogs are documented
Design tokens and spacing are mapped to the Console UI kit and a component list for dev handoff exists
deliverables: Design handoff doc with component specs + variants (link in Confluence PRD)
Interaction spec (keyboard, mouse, screenreader notes) attached to PRD
source.design_notes:
Iteration 1 used a vertical stepper; feedback from Eng & PM indicated it felt too modal and hid global context. Iteration 2 introduces a horizontal timeline with incident heatmap overlay and floating canary lane controls. Trade-offs: timeline gives better context for multi-cluster scheduling but is more complex to implement - Eng estimates a ~3-week frontend effort (ENG-19911).

Accessibility: color scale for incident heatmap must not rely on hue alone; we propose pattern fills + text labels for high/medium/low. Keyboarded timeline selection uses start/end input fields plus arrow-stepper controls.

Telemetry & UX metrics: track scheduling attempts, warnings dismissed, abort/rollback click rate, and time between schedule and first failure. These will feed a follow-up PM ticket to tune suggested windows by observed failure rates.
source.mockups_summary: Screen A: 'Select clusters' + cluster health strip (node availability, recent alerts). Screen B: Horizontal timeline with incident heatmap; drag handles to select window; 'Suggest safe windows' button that overlays 3 recommended slots. Screen C: Canary config panel (wave 1: 5% nodes in region A, wave 2: 25%, wave 3: 100%) with immediate preview of duration + expected impact. Screen D: Confirmation modal with audit note field and option to 'Schedule and Notify' or 'Dry-run (no rollout)'.
source.risks_and_mitigations:
Risk: Operators may ignore warnings and schedule during incidents. Mitigation: Add mandatory friction for high-risk schedules (two-step confirmation) and require a comment to proceed.
Risk: Large multi-cluster rollouts could cross-region dependencies. Mitigation: Add explicit dependency check with a visual badge and block scheduling if conflicts found (falls under ENG-19802).
comments: 2025-02-12 - Ava Chen: Initial draft and wireframes uploaded to Figma. Shared with PM (Samir) and eng leads for sizing.
2025-02-14 - Samir Patel (PM): Love the timeline but ask we add 'maintenance window templates' that teams can reuse. Added as follow-up (PM-4187).
2025-02-16 - Priya Narang (SRE): Request: show node-level rollback targets (allow rollback to previous kernel image) — flagged as out-of-scope for this ticket, forwarded to ENG-20010.
2025-02-18 - Miguel Torres (Design, assignee): Updated prototype with keyboard flows and added high-contrast patterns for heatmap. Pushed final mockups to PRD.

2025-02-19 - Elena Liu (Engineering): Reviewed interaction; noted complexity around simultaneous abort across regions. Suggested adding a single 'Abort rollout' API hook and a disabled UI path if backend lacks atomic abort. Filed dependency ENG-19911.

2025-02-20 - Design review session notes: Stakeholders approved timeline approach pending small copy changes and adding a 'preview impact' chart. Action items: update microcopy (Ava), add CSV export sample (Miguel), and confirm RBAC states with Security (Alice Wong).
source.implementation_notes: Frontend: component list includes TimelineCanvas, CanaryLane, ClusterHealthStrip, ConfirmationModal. Use existing Console design tokens. Backend: needs endpoints for suggested windows, incident overlays, dry-run simulation, schedule create/update, and abort. Eng tickets: ENG-19802 (dependency checks), ENG-19911 (abort orchestration), ENG-20010 (node rollback options).

source.title: Privileged role grant review & audit UX for Console
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Admins need a clear, low-friction way to grant, review, and revoke privileged roles (e.g., system-admin, billing-admin, oncall-privileged) while ensuring every change is auditable and can be justified for compliance. Current console path buries audit metadata and the member assignment flow creates accidental over-grants because scopes and resource boundaries are not surfaced.

Goal: Design a compact role grant review surface in Console that: 1) surfaces resource scoping and active sessions for the member being assigned; 2) flags high-risk grants with inline justifications and mandatory commit notes for P0/P1 roles; 3) integrates with audit log links and one-click revoke/impersonate for validation; 4) preserves quick bulk workflows for SRE/platform teams while preventing accidental permanent elevation.

Scope: UI/UX for role editor and member assignment flows only (Console). Non-goals: backend policy engine changes, SSO mapping logic, or RBAC enforcement semantics (these are separate eng tickets).

Key UX principles: minimal friction for routine ops, mandatory friction for high-risk actions, continuous discoverability of audit context, and clear affordances for temporary grants and review windows.
acceptance_criteria: Design includes a ‘Grant Review’ flyout that appears before finalizing member assignment for any role marked privileged.

Flyout must show: role privileges summary, affected resources (scoped projects/orgs), active sessions for the assignee, last 3 grants for this role in the org with links to audit entries, and a required justification field for P0/P1 roles.
UI includes an option to make the grant temporary (TTL) with suggested durations and automated expiry reminder tasks in Console.
Design artifacts (Figma) annotated with copy and error states, and handoff tokens for implementation team.
Accessibility review notes included (keyboard-first, screen reader text for audit links).
Design approved by PM (Linnea Sørensen) and Security (Rajiv Menon) and passed initial usability run with 5 internal engineers.
Final screens and component spec uploaded to design system; dev tickets created with links to mockups and acceptance criteria.
tasks: Research: compile top accidental over-grant incidents from incidents dashboard (ENG-ops to share) - due 2025-02-14
Sketch flows: initial 3 concepts for grant review flyout - design - due 2025-02-16
Prototype: high-fidelity Figma with interactions and audit links - due 2025-02-19
Usability: 5 quick interviews with platform engineers and one security reviewer - complete by 2025-02-20
Handoff: annotate tokens and create engineering tickets (ENG-41202, ENG-41203) - due 2025-02-21
comments: 2025-02-12 Ava Chen: Kickoff notes. Spoke with Rajiv (Security) and Linnea (PM). Security wants mandatory justification for system-admin grants and an approval step for org-level scope. PM requests TTL suggestions for temporary on-call elevation. Logged relevant incidents: INC-2024-09-14 (accidental billing-admin grant).

2025-02-14 Marcus Patel: Research update. ENG provided grant frequency metrics. ~12% of role assignments for privileged roles are done outside of change window; we should surface 'last grant' history in the flyout. Will create a lightweight API endpoint for 'recent-grants' to avoid heavy queries.

2025-02-16 Linnea Sørensen (PM): Reviewed sketches. Prefer the compact flyout with two-column layout: left for role summary + scope, right for audit history + justification. Add inline tooltip explaining TTL behavior and automated reminders.

2025-02-17 Rajiv Menon (Security): Approval flow: for organization-scoped system-admin grants, require either SSO group mapping or a second approver from Security. Also request a required audit tag field (freeform) that surfaces in logs. Note: mark security_review_required=true.

2025-02-18 Usability: 5 engineers tested prototype. Feedback: a) auto-suggest justification from template helped; b) TTL default of 8 hours was too short for on-call runbooks; propose presets 8h, 24h, 7d. c) need clearer affordance for bulk assignments with per-member override.
2025-02-19 Marcus Patel: Prototype updated. Figma link below. Created ENG-41202 (frontend) and ENG-41203 (audit-linking backend). Added QA ticket QA-998 for regression on member list performance.

2025-02-20 Ava Chen: Submitted for design review. Pending security sign-off on approver workflow and wording of mandatory justification. Next: finalize copy and create handoff notes for design system components.

source.title: Onboarding network topology journey frames
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Create a set of visual assets and annotated UI frames that guide operators through Private/Private-network onboarding end-to-end: topology overview, initial install steps, control-plane handshake, RBAC & key setup, and in-place upgrade flows. Assets will be used in the Console onboarding flow, docs quickstart, and release notes for runtime-1.18.
source.background: Current onboarding visuals are fragmented across docs and older flows assume a single cloud provider. For Private deployments customers frequently ask for clear network handoff diagrams and an upgrade modal flow that explains node draining and compatibility checks. This ticket consolidates visual language and produces replaceable screenshot assets and editable Figma components.
deliverables: Topology journey storyboard: 6 frames (overview, control-plane placement, customer network boundaries, ingress/egress, dataplane examples, fallback routing)
Install step artboards: 5 step-by-step screens matching Console copy and CLI snippets
Upgrade UI set: modal states for ‘check compatibility’, ‘drain & migrate’, ‘success’, ‘rollback’
Annotated screenshots for docs (PNG at 2x + source Figma frames)
Accessible SVG illustrations for docs header and empty states
visual_specifications: Figma file: Private-Onboarding/2025/Network-Journey (use existing component library)
Screen sizes: 1440x900 artboards for Console; 1200px wide hero illustrations for docs
File formats: SVG (illustrations), PNG 2x (screenshots), JPG preview (docs thumbnails)
Color tokens: use --brand-ink-700 for emphasis, --support-amber-500 for warnings
Typography: Redwood Console tokens (Inter variable). Headings H3/H4 per Console scale
Accessibility: ensure contrast ratio >= 4.5:1 for text overlays and 3:1 for UI hints
acceptance_criteria: All six topology storyboard frames completed and reviewed by PM and Eng
Install step screens match copy in PRD and have corresponding CLI snippet images
Upgrade modal states interactive prototype in Figma with annotated transition notes
Assets exported to docs/drive folder with naming convention: onboarding_network_<type>_<state>@2x.png
Design review sign-off recorded and a GH issue opened for Console implement with linked assets
progress_updates: 2025-02-14: Kickoff meeting with PM (Oliver Grant) and Infra. Collected existing diagrams and CLI snippets.
2025-02-16: First-pass topology sketches completed. Decided to replace cloud-provider icons with abstract boundary shapes.
2025-02-18: Prototype upgrade modal transitions in Figma. Eng requested additional ‘paused with warnings’ state.
2025-02-19: Added accessibility checklist and began exports for docs team. Seeking design review on 2025-02-20.
decisions: Use neutral, provider-agnostic visual metaphors for customer network boundaries to avoid implying vendor lock-in
Show a simplified ‘control-plane handshake’ animation concept (GIF in docs) but deliver static frames first
Make upgrade modal prioritize clear rollback messaging and show estimated downtime as a numeric field
next_steps: Finalize frame 4 (ingress/egress) with ingress controller variants by 2025-02-21
Export annotated screenshots and hand off to docs for copy alignment by 2025-02-22
Open GH issue for Console implementation and attach exported assets by 2025-02-23

source.title: Batching & Cache Setup Wizard — onboarding flow and safety guardrails
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design an end-to-end onboarding wizard inside Redwood Console that helps engineers and platform owners configure batching and KV/prefix caching for routes and model profiles. The wizard should: 1) quickly recommend safe defaults based on measured workload signals (median seq len, token rate, request size distribution), 2) explain trade-offs (latency vs cost vs variance) with short contextual explainers, 3) provide a simulated cost & latency preview for chosen presets and slider settings, 4) expose advanced controls in a scoped ‘expert’ panel with JSON preview and validation, and 5) enforce guardrails and warnings for settings that would violate SLOs or exceed estimated spend. This ticket covers the visual flows, microcopy, onboarding checklist, one progressive disclosure prototype in Figma, and a short pilot usability test with 6 backend engineers and 4 ML infra users.
acceptance_criteria: Onboarding wizard screens created in Figma covering: intro, workload import, recommendation, preview, expert edits, review & apply
Recommendation engine UI communicates confidence and input signals used (e.g., median seq len, 95th percentile QPS)
Simulated preview shows per-100k token cost estimate and median/95th latency estimate for selected profile
Guardrail warnings shown for configurations that risk increased 95th pct latency or >20% cost increase vs baseline
Advanced JSON editor available with inline validation and link to documentation
Prototype tested with at least 6 users and documented usability notes and prioritized changes
design_notes: Keep primary path under 5 steps to avoid flow drop-off for new users
Use progressive disclosure: simple presets (Conservative, Balanced, Aggressive) default to Balanced
Presets map to batching window, max batch size, KV prefix TTL, KV cache size, and fallback model variant
Preview should be conservative: show ranges and confidence bands rather than single-point estimates
Include small inline helper for each slider with threshold example (e.g., 'Batch window: 20–50ms suitable for QPS < 50')
Design must surface origin of workload signals (client-provided, recent request sample, estimated) — critical for trust
decisions: Use a wizard rather than inline modal so users can step back to previous inputs without losing state
Expose only three high-level presets on the default path; advanced controls behind a clearly labeled 'Expert options' toggle
Simulated preview will not call the runtime but use sampled telemetry + static cost model to avoid accidental provisioning
Guardrails will be soft by default (warnings) with an explicit acknowledgment required for dangerous combinations
All telemetry-sourced inputs must show timestamp and window (e.g., 'based on last 6 hours') to avoid confusion
comments: 2025-01-16: Kickoff with product and infra. Agreed to emphasize 'confidence' metric in recommendations. - Ava
2025-01-22: First round of mocks uploaded to Figma. Engineering asked to add a non-blocking option to 'save as draft' to let teams calibrate before applying. - Mateo
2025-01-29: Conducted 6 usability sessions (4 infra, 2 backend). Key pain: users misunderstood 'batch window' units. Action: add microcopy and an example visualizer. - Research
2025-01-31: Security requested explicit mention that KV TTL values are advisory for the runtime; will require follow-up with runtime team. Added ENG-2910 to dependencies. - Ava
2025-02-02: Updated prototype to include simulated 95th pct latency band and cost delta vs baseline. Need PM sign-off on preset label copy. - Mateo
2025-02-03: Design review scheduled with Console PM and Runtime leads. Next: implement clickable prototype and write handoff notes for engineering. - Mateo
notes_for_engineering: Provide telemetry API endpoint to fetch last-N hours request distribution and median seq length for the selected route
Expose cost model inputs (token cost per model, GPU token/sec assumptions) as constants in the console repo so preview matches billing
Validation should check config against route SLOs and return structured warnings that UI can render
Persist 'draft' configs scoped to team until user clicks 'apply'
Expert JSON edits require schema validation against runtime config schema

The wizard should: 1) quickly recommend safe defaults based on measured workload signals (median seq len, token rate, request size distribution), 2) explain trade-offs (latency vs cost vs variance) with short contextual explainers, 3) provide a simulated cost & latency preview for chosen presets and slider settings, 4) expose advanced controls in a scoped ‘expert’ panel with JSON preview and validation, and 5) enforce guardrails and warnings for settings that would violate SLOs or exceed estimated spend. This ticket covers the visual flows, microcopy, onboarding checklist, one progressive disclosure prototype in Figma, and a short pilot usability test with 6 backend engineers and 4 ML infra users.

source.title: Surface-elevation press shadows and RTL icon offsets: cross-theme button parity
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Audit and implement a coherent approach to button press feedback (shadows/contrast) that respects surface elevation tokens across light, dark, and high-contrast themes, and fix inconsistent RTL icon offsets and hit targets introduced during the recent token migration. Deliver a small set of token mappings, design specs, and a developer-friendly migration guide so engineering can implement with minimal regressions.
acceptance_criteria: Visual parity: primary/secondary/ghost/icon-only buttons show consistent press shadow or contrast change based on semantic elevation tokens for light, dark, and high-contrast themes
RTL parity: icon-only and icon-leading/leading-icon text buttons mirror horizontal offsets and padding so icon visual center aligns with LTR counterparts
Accessibility: focus ring remains visible for icon-only buttons across themes; keyboard activation does not lose visual feedback; motion-reduced users receive non-animated feedback
Token migration: new semantic tokens (surface-elevation-1/2/3, press-shadow, press-outline) are documented and mapped to legacy tokens with fallbacks so runtime rollouts don't regress appearance
Regression verification: QA checklist demonstrates fixes for at least three reproducible regressions reported by product/design in last sprint
Developer handoff: Figma spec file, token table, and a short PR checklist are available in the linked artifacts
comments: 2025-01-10 - Ava Chen: Created ticket after noticing visual drift in dark theme during token migration. Initial audit attached.
2025-01-11 - Sofia Martinez: Scoped to cover press shadows, RTL offsets, keyboard focus visibility, and migration docs. Estimate set to 3 points.
2025-01-13 - Mateo Ruiz (Eng): Dev notes - existing runtime uses a single press-overlay color that flattens in dark mode; need separate semantic shadow token rather than color-only approach.
2025-01-14 - Priya Nair (A11y): Accessibility review scheduled. Requesting ARIA guidance for icon-only buttons and explicit focus token mapping for high-contrast mode.
2025-01-16 - Sofia Martinez: Implemented design tests in Figma: added elevation token variants and motion-reduced behaviors. Uploaded comp library to the team file.
2025-01-17 - QA: Found intermittent loss of focus ring when toggling from LTR to RTL on Safari; added to dev acceptance criteria.
2025-01-18 - Ava Chen: Updated ticket with links to PR and design specs. Ready for engineering sprint allocation.
source.notes: Trade-offs discussed: use subtle shadow on dark surfaces to avoid halo on very dark backgrounds vs. using stronger outline in high-contrast mode. Chosen approach uses semantic shadow token plus a high-contrast outline token to cover both cases. Dev to provide toggle flag for early rollout.
